I’m heads-up in the small blind with Q♦5♣. I already completed to 100 earlier; Flash just raised to 400 so I must call 300 more. Q5o is marginal but heads‑up it has reasonable equity against a wide raise. Calling 300 into a 500 pot gives ~2.67:1 pot odds (≈27% equity needed) and the cost is small relative to my 3,840 stack. I don’t want to 3‑bet out of position with this hand — that would commit too many chips with poor postflop playability. I’ll call to 400 total and plan to fold to heavy postflop aggression if I miss, and continue only with top pair, two pair/sets, or strong draws.

I missed the flop. I hold Q♦5♣ on 8♦ 6♠ 6♥ — no pair, no real draws, out of position vs the preflop raiser. The board is paired and likely hits a lot of the raiser’s range (6x, 8x, overpairs, sets, etc.), and I have few meaningful blockers to make a bluff credible. Pot is 800 and my stack is 3,540; betting here would bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand and invite a shove or raise that I should fold to. I’ll check and preserve my stack — fold to any sizeable barrel. I may call a very small probe (very small sizing) for price/information, but otherwise check-fold.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit on a paired board of 8-6-6. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is the standard play here. This board doesn't hit many hands in a standard limp-call range except for perhaps some medium pairs or the occasional 8. By betting, I maintain my initiative and can often win the pot immediately if Minni missed the flop. A bet of 300 into the 800 pot is a standard sizing to apply pressure without over-risking.

I have Q♦5♣ on 8♦-6♠-6♥ — I completely missed (no pair, no realistic draws). Flash (the preflop raiser) bets 300 into a 1,100 pot. Calling 300 costs only ~8.5% of my 3,540 stack and gives pot odds of ~21% (300/(1,100+300) ≈ 21.4%), while my chance to pair by the river with 6 outs is ~24%. The bet is a fairly small probe from a wide raiser, so calling is a cheap way to realize equity and gather information. Plan: call to see the turn, but fold to a sizeable turn barrel unless I improve to at least a pair (or a very strong secondary draw); I won't bloat the pot out of position with a weak hand.

Check. The turn pairs the board (8♦ 6♠ 6♥ 8♣) and I hold Q♦5♣ with no pair and no realistic draw. Any 8 or 6 in Flash’s range now converts to a full house, and a lot of his preflop-raising range (pairs/overpairs/6x/8x) beats me. I have only 6 outs to pair by the river (3 queens + 3 fives) — 6/46 ≈ 13% — and even if I hit I can easily still be behind to a full house. Pot is 1,400 and my stack 3,240; there’s no value or fold equity in betting here and bluffs on a double‑paired static board are thin. I’ll check and see the river; fold to any significant bet, consider calling a very small probe if the price is correct, and only continue aggressively if I actually pair or the river creates a credible counterstory.

The board has double-paired (8-6-6-8), meaning everyone currently has at least two pair. I have Ace-high, which gives me the best possible kicker for those two pair unless Minni also has an Ace. Minni limp-called pre-flop and has check-called the flop. While I could bet for value against some King-high hands, most hands that call a second barrel here likely have me beaten (a 6, an 8, or a pocket pair). Checking back allows me to control the pot size and utilize my showdown value, as I am beating all of Minni's 'air' and missing ranges. It also potentially induces a bluff on the river which I can evaluate then.

I have Q♦5♣ on 8♦ 6♠ 6♥ 8♣ K♦ — I missed completely. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop; the turn made the board double‑paired, so his range is heavily weighted toward full houses/trips (any 6x/8x, K6/K8, pocket pairs, even quads in rare cases). My river showdown equity is essentially zero and a bluff has very thin fold equity against this line. Pot is 1,400 and I have 3,240 — no reason to invest chips with a busted hand. I’ll check and preserve my stack, folding to any meaningful bet.

The board is double-paired (8-6-6-8), meaning all players currently hold at least two pair. My hand is Ace-Queen, which gives me the best possible kicker (Ace) for the two pair on the board. On the river, a King appeared. If Minni has a King, she has a better two pair (K-K-8-8). If she has an 8 or a 6, she has a full house. Minni has checked to me on the river. While my Ace-high is strong, betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands (since most hands I beat would fold) and would likely be called or raised by better hands (Kings, 6s, or 8s). Checking allows me to see a showdown and potentially win or split the pot without further risk.

I'm on the button with 8♣5♥ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Stacks are ~32BB for me and both opponents are deep and capable; calling 100 would likely put me in a multiway, dominated or low-equity spot postflop. Raising as a pure steal is risky here — a small raise will be called too often and a larger raise can bring 3-bets from deep stacks where my hand performs poorly. With plenty of chips left and many better spots to pick off the blinds, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a stronger hand or a clearer steal opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-3 offsuit. Minni has folded on the button, leaving only Claude in the Big Blind. While completing the bet is only 50 chips, 5-3 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands and plays very poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and there's no need to force a play with trash cards, folding is the most disciplined and standard decision here.

I am on the button with K6o. This is a standard opening situation in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy and Minni is very short-stacked in the Big Blind. Raising here allows me to pick up the blinds frequently or play a pot in position. Given Minni's short stack, I should be cautious if they jam, but K6o is strong enough to open for a standard raise.

I'm in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 from Flash. With K6o, I have a marginal but playable hand. Key factors: (1) Getting decent pot odds - 450:250, or 1.8:1 (2) Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament, likely testing from the button (3) I have a healthy 17,760 chip stack to work with postflop (4) Minni is very short at 3,140 - likely to fold most hands (5) Small blind position is a standard defense position against button aggression. K6o is weak but has some showdown value and two live cards. I should call here to see a flop with decent odds, good position if we get heads-up, and the ability to make postflop decisions based on board texture.

Stack 3,140 (~31 BB). Facing a raise to 300 from the button and a small‑blind call; pot is 700 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total commitment this street). Holding A♦3♦ — a suited ace with decent playability and good pot odds here (200 to win 900 if I call). I’m out of position to both opponents, so a 3‑bet or shove is unattractive: it’s likely to get called by better hands or isolate me out of position against deep stacks. I’ll call to see a flop cheaply and look to continue only if I hit an ace/diamond draw or get a favorable board; fold to large aggression on dry boards.

On the flop with K6o against two opponents, I'm in a weak position. My holding is K-high with a backdoor club flush draw - marginal at best. Key factors: (1) Out of position - I'm first to act, which is a disadvantage with a weak hand. (2) Hand strength - K-high is vulnerable to Flash's likely range from the button. (3) Stack depth consideration - Minni is critically short at 2,940, making his range very wide if he plays. (4) By checking, I keep the pot manageable and allow Flash to show their hand strength. (5) The 4-3-9 board texture is dry and not particularly favorable for my holdings. (6) I can control pot size when out of position by checking and deciding on subsequent streets based on action. Checking gives me flexibility to evaluate Flash's response and Minni's commitment before investing more chips with a weak hand.

I have A♦3♦ and flopped a pair of threes on 4♣ 3♣ 9♥. Claude checked and the original raiser (Flash) acts after me — I’m out of position to the likely c-bettor. This is a marginal made hand: no club draw for me and with two diamonds in hand a diamond flush is no longer possible, so my equity is limited. Betting here will often get called by better pairs (9x, 4x) or induce a check‑raise; I prefer pot control and to see how the button acts. I’ll check, plan to call small–medium c‑bets, and fold or seriously re‑evaluate against large, polarizing raises.

I have King high on a board of 4-3-9 with two clubs. Since I am in a three-way pot and both opponents have checked to me, checking back is a safe and standard play. I have some showdown value with King high, but little equity to continue if I am raised or called. Checking allows me to see a turn card for free and keep the pot small, which is beneficial given Minni's short stack and the potential for Claude to be slow-playing or holding a draw.

Analyzing the turn situation:

Board: 4c 3c 9h 5c
My hand: 6c Kh (K-6 offsuit)
Pot: 900 | To call: 0 | Position: Small Blind (first to act on turn)

Hand Assessment:
- Made hand: King-high only - very weak
- Draws: Open-ended straight draw (any 2 or 7 gives me a straight = 8 outs), plus backdoor club flush draw (9 remaining clubs)
- Despite having some drawing possibilities, this is a marginal hand at best

Strategic Analysis:
1. I'm out of position against Flash, who has shown aggression throughout the tournament
2. The flop check around (all three of us checked) suggests no one connected strongly
3. Minni is critically short-stacked (2,940), so he's not a major threat
4. Checking the flop was correct, and continuing to check the turn maintains pot control
5. With K-high and only drawing possibilities, I shouldn't try to represent strength

If I bet here: I'm bluffing with very weak showdown value. Flash can call with a wide range or even re-raise, and I'd be in a difficult spot with just draws.

If I check: I keep the pot small, see Flash's action, and retain fold equity. If Flash checks back, I get a free river with my draws. If Flash bets, I can fold without losing unnecessary chips on a weak hand.

Given my weak hand strength, out-of-position disadvantage, and need to preserve chips, checking is the optimal play.

Check. I have A♦3♦ — only a marginal one‑pair (pair of threes with an Ace kicker). The turn (4♣ 3♣ 9♥ 5♣) brings a third club, so any club in an opponent’s hand is now a made flush and my hand is highly vulnerable. There are two deep opponents (button raised preflop, SB called) so their ranges include many club combos; both checked the flop and Claude checked the turn, which reduces but doesn’t eliminate the chance of a strong holding or slowplay. Betting into two players with this hand risks getting raised or isolating myself out of position and bloating the pot with a weak pair. Pot control is the priority: I’ll check to keep the pot manageable and see how they act. Plan: fold to a large, polarizing turn bet or check‑raise; call small/standard-sized bets (roughly ≤ ~1/3 pot) to see a river; if checks through to a relatively safe river (non‑club, non‑card that completes obvious straights), reassess for a small value bet versus single opponents or check back to reach showdown.

I have the King of Clubs (Kc), which gives me the second-best possible flush draw on this three-club board. I also have a gutshot straight draw. Since both opponents have checked to me on the turn after a checked flop, I have significant showdown value with King-high and plenty of equity to improve on the river. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity without risking a check-raise from Claude or a desperate shove from the short-stacked Minni. If I hit my flush or straight, I can value bet the river; if I miss, I might still win at showdown or consider a bluff depending on the river card and action.

River analysis with K6o on 4c-3c-9h-5c-7s: I have just King-high, a very weak hand. Key factors: (1) The entire hand has been extremely passive - all three players checked through flop and turn, suggesting no one connected strongly. (2) Out of position, I act first on the river. (3) With King-high and marginal showdown value against two opponents, betting is a thin bluff that accomplishes little. (4) Minni is critically short at 2,940 chips - he'll likely call with any hand if still interested in the pot. (5) Flash, who raised pre-flop, could have anything but the passive action suggests a marginal hand. (6) If I bet, I'm unlikely to win the pot without showdown value, and if called or check-raised, I'm in a difficult spot. (7) If Flash bets, I'll have to make a tough decision with King-high. By checking, I retain the option to fold to aggressive action, minimize losses with a weak hand, and see showdown if Flash also checks. This is the prudent line with minimal equity.

I have A♦3♦ — a weak one-pair (trip 3s? no, just pair of threes) with a good kicker but on a wet-looking board: 4♣ 3♣ 9♥ 5♣ 7♠. There are three clubs on board (possible flushes) and a 6 would have completed a 3-4-5-6-7 straight, so my hand is very vulnerable to many hands in the opponents’ ranges. Both opponents checked through earlier streets, indicating passivity or medium-strength holdings; betting now would likely get called by better hands (9x, 7x, 4x, sets, flushes) or fold out very few worse combos (only deuce combos and rare weaker one-pairs). With the pot already small (900) and my pair marginal, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and take showdown rather than risk a raise or bloating the pot. I’ll check to river showdown and avoid getting raised off my marginal hand.

On the river, the board is 4c-3c-9h-5c-7s. My 6h completes a 7-high straight (3-4-5-6-7). While there are three clubs on the board, the action has been checked through the flop and turn, and both opponents have checked the river to me. This passive play makes it unlikely that either Claude or Minni has a flush or a higher straight. I will place a value bet of 500 into the 900 pot, aiming to get called by a pair of nines, a seven, or perhaps a lower straight.

River decision on board 4c-3c-9h-5c-7s with K6o: I'm facing a 500-chip river bet into a 1400-chip pot. My hand is King-high only - extremely weak with zero made value. Analysis: (1) Pot odds requirement: I need 500/(1400+500) = 26.3% equity to break even, but King-high has significantly less equity against Flash's river betting range. (2) Opponent assessment: Flash raised preflop from button (suggesting a decent hand initially) but then checked both the flop and turn. The delayed river bet could indicate either a made hand that improved on the river or a bluff attempt. (3) Hand strength: King-high has marginal to poor showdown value. I beat only complete air, missed draws, and hands worse than King-high - a narrow range. (4) Positional disadvantage: I act first on the river, limiting my information. (5) Board texture: The 4-3-9-5-7 board is quite dry with no obvious flush or straight possibilities. Flash's checkback on flop/turn followed by a river bet suggests either a hand that rivers value or a steal attempt, but either way, King-high is dominated by most of Flash's likely holdings. This is a clear fold to preserve chips with a hand that lacks the equity to justify a call.

Board is 4♣‑3♣‑9♥‑5♣‑7♠, I hold A♦3♦ (a single pair, threes, with an Ace kicker). Flash (BTN) opened preflop, checked flop and turn and now bets a small 500 into the pot (pot shown 1400) — Claude folded, so it’s heads‑up. That bet size and the passive line suggest a mixed river range: some clear value (9x, 7x, 5x, sets, and possible club flushes) but also a fair number of missed hands / thin bluffs. Calling 500 to potentially win 1900 requires ~26% equity. My pair of threes will beat many bluffs and weaker one‑pairs, and a 500 call is only ~5BB out of my ~29BB stack, so the risk is reasonable. A raise to the min (1000) would commit too much with a marginal hand and get called by most value hands; folding would deny good pot odds against bluffs. I’ll call and take this to showdown.

Calling from the button with Ts5h. The 2.5:1 pot odds combined with positional advantage justify seeing a flop. Minni's short stack (2,390) and my stack depth create opportunities for post-flop aggression. Position is crucial in this spot - I'll be able to act last and make informed decisions on later streets.

Claude (button) just limped and the pot is 250; I need to put in 50 more (100 total committed preflop). I have J9o in the small blind with ~24bb — a marginal but playable hand in 3‑way. Raising to 200 would put me out of position and invite wide 3‑bets from Flash (BB), so I prefer to see a flop cheaply. I'll call to keep the pot manageable and proceed postflop only with a pair, strong draw, or a clear fold vs heavy action. If Flash 3‑bets big preflop I'll fold most of the time given the stack depth and hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 8c. Both the Button (Claude) and the Small Blind (Minni) have limped into the pot. While Kc 8c is a relatively strong hand compared to a limping range, Minni is playing a very short stack (23.4 BB). If I raise here, I risk a shove from her that I would likely have to fold to, losing equity. By checking, I see a flop for free with a hand that has good playability and nut potential (flushes).

Flop: Qh Qs Kd. My hand Jd9c has missed — no pair, no realistic flush, only a 4‑out gutshot to a T for a straight (~16% to river). With two opponents (one limped, one checked) the range is wide but the chance someone has a K or a Q (or a pair they will defend) is substantial. I called preflop to see a cheap flop and planned to continue only with a pair or a strong draw; I don’t have either. Betting as a bluff into two players with deep stacks risks a call/raise and commits too much of my ~23.4BB for little equity. I’ll check and see a free turn; if faced with significant aggression I will fold, and if I hit the Ten I can take a more aggressive line.

I have top pair (Kings) with an 8 kicker on a paired board (Q-Q-K). In a limped pot, this is a very strong hand. However, given the paired board and the fact that Minni is playing a short stack, checking is a prudent move to control the size of the pot and see how the action develops. It also avoids potentially facing a check-shove from Minni if she has a Queen or a draw. I'll check and see what the Button (Claude) does.

With Ts5h on a board of Qh-Qs-Kd, I have no made hand, no draws, and essentially air. Both Minni and Flash have checked to me, indicating they don't hold strong hands. Betting here would be a mistake - I'd be bluffing without the board texture or stack dynamics to justify it. Minni's short stack (2,340) doesn't create an exploitable spot when both opponents have checked. The best play is to check and see the turn card. If I improve or the turn presents better opportunities, I can reassess. Bluffing into two opponents with a weak hand and no fold equity is not profitable.

Board: Qh Qs Kd Qd — trips on board. I hold Jd9c with ~23.4bb and have no pair or meaningful draw. There’s no value in bluffing into two deep stacks on a very wet/paired board where hands that beat me (Ks, any Q for quads, pocket pairs) are well within opponents’ ranges. Pot is small relative to stacks and checking preserves chips; I will check and fold to any serious bet. If the street checks through to the river I can reassess with the river card, but I won’t build the pot here with air.

I have quad Queens on the board with a King kicker. The board is now Qh-Qs-Kd-Qd. Since there is only one Queen left in the deck, and I hold a King, I have an extremely strong hand (quads with a King kicker). The only hand that beats me is exactly the case Ace of Queens (for quads with an Ace kicker). Minni has checked, and the pot is small (300). With Minni having a very short stack (2340), I want to extract value without folding out Claude. However, a check here is likely best to see if Claude attempts a bluff or if the river brings a card that allows me to get paid. If I bet now, I likely fold out everything except an Ace or the last Queen. I will check to induce action from the Button.

With Ts5h on a Qh-Qs-Kd-Qd board, I have complete air. Both opponents checked the turn, but attempting a bluff into two players without strong fold equity is not profitable. I check and take a free card to the river, where I can reassess based on the final board and any action from my opponents. This conservative line preserves my stack in a hand where I have no equity.

The river gives me Jd in hand with Jh on board, so I have a full house: Queens full of Jacks (QQQJJ). That’s a very strong showdown hand. Hands that can beat me are rare here — the remaining queen (quads), any king in hand (QQQKK), or a higher paired hole-card (e.g. AA) — but all three players checked through the flop and turn, making those monstrous holdings less likely (they’d often have taken a stab earlier or checked-raise). The pot is only 300 and my stack is ~2,340, so a small-to-medium value bet is appropriate to extract from worse full houses, single J’s, and medium pocket pairs while keeping risk manageable. I’ll bet for thin value (about 2x the pot). If I face a significant raise I will fold — the raising range here is weighted toward the few hands that beat me.

The board is Qh-Qs-Kd-Qd-Jh, which means there are three Queens on the board. Holding Kc-8c, my best 5-card hand is a Full House: Queens full of Kings (Q-Q-Q-K-K). While this is a very strong hand, it is not the absolute nuts. I am beaten by the case Queen (Qc) which would make Quad Queens, or pocket Aces (AA) which would make Queens full of Aces. Given that this was a limped pot that checked all the way to the river, it is possible someone is slow-playing a monster, particularly the Queen. Minni's lead for 600 into a 300 pot (a 2x pot bet) is quite polar—it's often either a total bluff or a very strong hand. With Claude still to act behind me, calling is the most prudent move. It allows me to see a showdown for a relatively small price while protecting against a potential trap from either opponent. If I raise, I risk being shoved on by a better hand, and I likely fold out anything that I currently beat.

Calling with three Queens (QQQ KJ). The betting pattern suggests weakness - both opponents checked the turn before Minni's river bet. With 4.5:1 pot odds and a strong made hand, this call is profitable. My hand beats all single pairs and most unpaired holdings. While full houses are possible (KK, JJ, KJ), the passive turn check makes this unlikely, particularly for both opponents. The risk-reward profile and equity against the likely range justifies the call.

I'm on the Button with 7♣6♠ and a 1,740 stack (~17.4 BB). In 3‑handed play this is a hand I want to be aggressive with because position gives me a big edge and 7‑6 has decent playability post‑flop. Folding is too tight; simply calling (limping) surrenders initiative and invites multiway action. I want fold equity and to narrow the field while keeping the pot manageable given my shortish stack, so I open‑raise to 400 (4 BB). That size pressures the blinds, preserves post‑flop maneuvering room, and keeps shove decisions avoidable. If I'm called I'll use position to c‑bet favorable flops and control pot size; if faced with a sizable 3‑bet I'll re‑evaluate (likely fold unless odds or opponent sizing justify a shove).

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of Sixes (6d 6h). Minni, the Button, has raised to 400. Minni is extremely short-stacked with only 1340 chips remaining (including the 400 already bet). Claude is in the Big Blind with a deep stack. Pocket sixes are a strong hand three-handed, especially against a short stack who is likely pushing wide to double up. My goal is to isolate Minni to see the showdown or take the pot. However, just calling allows Claude to come in for cheap. Raising to 1000 or even moving all-in is an option to isolate. Given Minni's stack size, if I raise to 1000, she only has 340 behind, making her effectively all-in anyway. I will raise to 1100 to force Claude out and commit Minni.

3c9c is a weak hand that doesn't warrant getting involved in a 3-bet pot from the big blind. Flash's raise to 1100 over Minni's 400 raise represents a strong hand, and I lack the equity needed to call. With 16,710 chips and 150 hands to play, I should preserve my stack by folding marginal hands preflop. I have better spots ahead.

I opened to 400 on the button with 7♣6♠ but Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1,100 — a sizable, value‑heavy sizing from a deep stack. I must call 700 into a 1,600 pot, which requires ~30% equity to break even. 7‑6 offsuit has well under that versus a typical SB 3‑bet range (pair and broadway heavy), and a shove to 1,340 only adds 240 more — giving Flash excellent odds to call. With poor equity, negligible fold equity on an all‑in and a short but still useful stack to wait for a better spot, the +EV play here is to release the hand and preserve chips.

I have King-Four suited on the button. Minni is extremely short-stacked in the Big Blind with only 1240 chips (12.4 big blinds). In this situation, I should be very aggressive to put pressure on both blinds, especially Minni who is essentially in 'push-or-fold' mode. A standard raise is likely to fold out Claude in the small blind, and I am happy to play against Minni's range or simply take down the blinds and antes.

I'm in the Small Blind with Td Kd - a premium broadway hand with flush draw potential. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and Minni is short-stacked at 1,240 in the big blind.

Key considerations:
1. Hand strength: Td Kd is a top-tier hand that should be played aggressively
2. Stack situation: Minni is critically short (1,240), making him a push-fold candidate. Flash has a large stack (21,650)
3. Pot odds: Getting ~1.8:1 to call, which is fine, but raising is stronger
4. Position dynamics: By raising from the small blind, I can potentially isolate Flash or fold out Minni

Strategy: A 3-bet is optimal here. Raising to 800 applies pressure, shows premium hand strength, and achieves several goals:
- Isolates Flash to a heads-up situation (likely)
- Folds Minni often due to short stack
- Builds the pot with a premium hand
- Takes control of the action

This is a standard aggressive play with a strong hand from early position.

Stack is short (1240 ≈ 12.4 BB) and I have A♣Q♥ in the big blind facing BTN open to 300 and an SB 3‑bet to 800 (pot 1200, to call 700). With ~12 BB a shove maximizes EV here — it gets fold equity to take down the 1200 pot immediately and avoids awkward OOP postflop decisions with a short stack. Calling would leave me with ~5.4 BB behind and allow the button/SB to play position and apply pressure; I also can’t complete a legal min‑raise to 1300 so the only way to apply maximum pressure is to go all‑in. AQs/AQo is strong enough vs typical 3‑bet ranges to shove for stacks this size. I will shove all‑in (commit my entire 1240) to maximize chance to double up or pick up the pot now.

Minni is all-in for her last 1300 chips. Claude has already committed 800 chips. With King-Four suited, the pot odds are attractive to see a flop. I need to call 1000 to win a pot that will be at least 2400. While my hand is not particularly strong, I have position for the remainder of the hand and Minni's range for an all-in from the big blind can be quite wide given her extremely short stack. Calling here keeps Claude in the pot and allows me to play the post-flop streets with position.

Calling with Td Kd is clearly the correct play. I have a premium broadway hand facing excellent pot odds of 6.8:1. Minni is all-in with a short stack (40 chips remaining), and Flash has committed to the pot. My hand is well-positioned against both opponents' likely ranges, and the risk/reward profile is highly favorable. This allows us to see the flop in a 3-way pot where my Td Kd should perform well against the typical holdings of short-stacked all-ins and aggressive calls.

With Td Kd on an As 2s 8d flop, I have a premium draw (13 diamond outs to the flush, plus broadway cards) but currently sit at king-high. The pot is 3900 with Minni all-in (40 chips) and Flash still in the hand with 20650 in his stack. Checking is the correct play here for several reasons: (1) Flash's preflop 4-bet call indicates significant hand strength, likely an overpair or ace-king type hand, making a flop bet vulnerable to being raised; (2) My flush draw gives me substantial equity (approximately 35-40% against most hands) while still being behind if Flash holds an ace; (3) Checking allows me to see another card with favorable pot odds if Flash bets - my draw is strong enough that I can profitably call another street; (4) If Flash checks behind, I improve my information and can reassess on the turn; (5) Pot control is important given the deep stacks. This is a classic semi-bluff situation where my draw equity is strong enough to justify calling a continuation bet, so checking doesn't cede control but rather plays the equity of my hand optimally.

I have top pair with A♣Q♥ on a dry Ace-high flop (A♠ 2♠ 8♦) but I'm effectively all-in with only 40 chips left and the pot is 3,900. A bet of 40 is negligible relative to the pot and will produce almost no fold equity, so betting now just commits my tiny remaining stack for little benefit. Checking preserves the chance to win the whole pot immediately if everyone checks, and if an opponent fires a bet I can still commit my 40 as an effective all-in. Given the stack-to-pot dynamics and board texture, checking is the higher-EV line.

The flop is Ace-high and I have King-high with no real connection to the board. Minni is effectively all-in, and Claude has checked to me. There is no reason to bet here as I have no value, and a bluff is unlikely to succeed given the pre-flop action where someone likely holds an Ace or a strong pair. I will check to see a free turn card and potentially improve or see how the side pot develops.

Turn: As 2s 8d 9h. Flash's flop check after a 4-bet preflop indicates weakness—unlikely AA/AK/AQ. Most probable holdings are TT/JJ/QQ/KK. My Td Kd has strong equity (approximately 45%) with a 4-card flush draw and broadway overcards. The 9h is a blank. Minni is all-in with 40 chips, making this essentially heads-up. Betting 1500 (38% of pot) applies pressure on Flash's apparent weakness, builds the pot with my draw equity, and forces him to make a difficult decision. This is superior to checking because: (1) it exploits his weakness, (2) it builds the pot when I'm in a good spot equity-wise, (3) it simplifies lines and gets value from my draw. If raised, I maintain options with 15.4k behind.

I have top pair with the best available kicker (A♣Q♥) on a fairly dry A‑2‑8‑9 board and I only have 40 chips left. Claude bet 1500 but my required call is just 40 into a 5,400 pot — the pot odds are enormous and this is effectively an all‑in for my remaining stack. Folding 40 into a pot this size with top pair would be a huge leak; I can’t raise with my stack anyway. I’ll call the 40 to see showdown and maximize EV.

On the turn with a board of As-2s-8d-9h, I have King-high (Kh 4h) with no draws. Pre-flop, there was significant action involving a 3-bet from Claude and a 4-bet all-in from Minni, suggesting strong ranges. Claude checked the flop but has now bet 1500 into the side pot on the turn. Since Minni is already all-in, I am only competing with Claude for the side pot and both of them for the main pot. Given the pre-flop strength shown and the Ace on the board, it is highly probable that Claude has at least a pair or an Ace, and even if he is bluffing, my King-high is unlikely to be the best hand. I have no equity to continue and should preserve my stack.

Folding 4-10 offsuit from the button. While position is valuable for stealing, this hand is too weak to play profitably. It lacks connectivity, suitedness, or significant high card value. Even with favorable pot odds of 1.5:1 (150 to call 100), the hand's poor equity against the likely calling range of the blinds (especially with Minni in a short stack situation and Flash with a decent stack) makes this a clear fold. Preserving chips for stronger opportunities is crucial when playing 150 hands with the goal of maximizing stack size.

I’m in the small blind with 3♥6♠ — a weak, unsuited hand and I’m out of position vs only the big blind. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call (I’ve already posted 50), so calling requires ~25% equity (50/(150+50)=25%). It’s too weak to open-raise and risking a 3-bet from a deep-stacked opponent is unnecessary; folding forfeits good pot odds. I’ll defend cheaply to see a flop and keep the pot manageable. If the BB shows significant aggression postflop I’ll fold unless I hit.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) completed the bet, which usually indicates a wide and often weak range of hands. With a significant chip lead and a hand that is well ahead of a typical limping range, I will raise to 400 to take initiative and put pressure on the short-stacked Small Blind.

I’m on the small blind with 3♥6♠ — a very weak, unsuited hand. Flash has raised to 400; I must call 300 into a 500 pot, which means I need ~37.5% equity (300/(500+300)). 3-6o has far less equity versus a standard raising range and I’ll be out of position postflop, so calling is a losing long-term play and 3-betting is unreasonable. I’ll preserve my 3,880 stack and fold this marginal spot.

Three-handed with me on the button. Q♠2♦ is a weak, easily-dominated hand, but I have positional advantage and decent stack (≈38.8bb). Pot is only 150 and I can raise small to try to steal the blinds. Limping invites multiway pots with a poor hand; folding is too nitty for the size of the pot. I’ll make a small, constructive raise to 200 to apply pressure and take the initiative. Plan: fold to any sizable 3-bet (Q2o is not a hand to defend big pots with), and if called proceed cautiously post-flop — check for pot control and only continue with clear equity (pair of queens, two pair, strong draw).

I am in the Small Blind with pocket Nines (9c, 9d), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Minni, who is the short stack with about 37 big blinds, opened from the Button for a min-raise to 200. Given my position and the strength of my hand, I want to 3-bet to isolate the button and take control of the pot. A 3-bet to 700 (3.5x the open) is a standard size that puts pressure on Minni's stack and forces the Big Blind (Claude) to have a very strong hand to continue. If Minni shoves over my raise, I am comfortably calling with 99 given the stack depths and the dynamics of three-handed play.

Folding 4h8c (one of the worst hands in poker) facing a 3-bet from Flash out of position. With weak hand strength, no draws, and facing two aggressive opponents in a 3-bet pot, this is a straightforward fold to preserve stack for better opportunities.

I opened to 200 on the button with Q♠2♦ to apply pressure, but Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 700. Q2o is easily dominated by a typical 3‑betting range and has poor post‑flop equity; calling 500 into a 1000 pot gives only ~25% break‑even price and is not attractive here. A 4‑bet to 1200 would be reckless with this holding. With ~36.8bb I’ll conserve chips and fold to this sizable 3‑bet and wait for a better spot in position.

I have King of Hearts and Queen of Clubs on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a very strong hand for this position. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. Minni is relatively short-stacked, so I need to be prepared for a potential shove, but KQo is easily strong enough to open here.

Calling 250 into a 450 pot with Ad7d from Small Blind. This hand has decent equity potential with an ace-high and flush draw possibilities, and the pot odds justify the call. Avoiding a marginal 3-bet while maintaining the option to play this hand in position postflop with my healthy 15k stack.

I'm in the big blind with Tc7s facing a button raise to 300 and a small‑blind call (pot ~700, I must call 200). T7o is a marginal hand that fares poorly in a 3‑way pot and will be dominated by most of the button's/caller's calling ranges. Although the pot odds (~4.5:1) are tempting, my postflop position is worst (acting first vs two opponents) and my equity vs two calling ranges is below the break‑even threshold. A small reraise to isolate is unlikely to fold out the opener and would put me OOP in a bigger pot with a marginal holding. With ~35BB and deeper stacks behind, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold this spot.

On the 8s Jh 5c flop, I have Ad7d - ace-high with a 4-card flush draw but no made hand. While I have some equity from the flush draw and ace kicker, my hand is currently weak. Flash's pre-flop raise from the button likely contains stronger holdings (overpairs, broadway cards, or hands like AK/AQ). Being out of position with a drawing hand, checking is the prudent approach. This allows me to see Flash's action and keep the pot small while I have weak equity. If Flash checks back, I gain a free card. If Flash bets, I can fold marginal or call with favorable odds depending on the bet size and remaining stack situations. Minni is very short-stacked at 3,580 and already folded pre-flop, so the dynamic is effectively two-handed.

I am the pre-flop aggressor holding KQo on a J-8-5 rainbow board. While I haven't hit a pair, I have two overcards and the board is relatively dry, making it a good candidate for a continuation bet. Claude checked to me, which often signifies weakness or a marginal hand. By betting around 40-45% of the pot, I can fold out better Ace-high hands or small pairs that might have missed, while still having equity to improve on the turn if called.

Calling 300 on the flop with Ad7d. I'm getting 4.33:1 pot odds with a 4-card flush draw (11 outs) plus ace-high, giving me approximately 35-40% equity. This is mathematically favorable compared to the 2.7:1 break-even odds for a flush draw. The pot will reach 1300, and I maintain position flexibility on the turn. If I hit the diamonds, I'm likely winning a sizable pot. If I miss, I can evaluate the turn action with my remaining 14,670 chips.

Checking with Ad7d on a 8s Jh 5c 3d board. I have a strong flush draw (10 outs) but currently just ace-high. Out of position against Flash who has shown aggression. Checking allows me to control pot size, see Flash's action cheaply, and potentially get a free river card. If Flash bets, I'll have favorable pot odds to call and complete my draw. This is the optimal play when drawing weak from out of position against an aggressive opponent.

On the turn 3d, which is a complete brick, Claude has checked again. My King-high currently has some showdown value against straight draws like 9-7 or T-9, but I am likely behind a Jack or even an Eight. Since Claude called my flop bet, he likely has a piece of the board. Checking behind allows me to see a free river card and potentially hit a King or Queen to take the lead, while also controlling the size of the pot and avoiding a potential check-raise.

River analysis: I'm holding Ad7d on a 8s Jh 5c 3d Kd board, giving me ace-high (Ad Kd Jh 8s 7d). My flush draw completely missed—I have only 4 diamonds visible, not the 5 needed for a hand. I'm out of position and my hand is weak. Flash's pre-flop raise from the button could contain strong holdings (AK, AQ, pairs), and while Flash checked the turn (suggesting a cautious hand or missed draw), I'm likely behind many of Flash's possible holdings. Betting with ace-high from out of position is unprofitable: I'll only win the 1300 pot if Flash folds, but Flash is more likely to call with any reasonable hand or simply check back. By checking, I either take down the pot for free if Flash checks (common given the turn check), or I avoid putting more chips at risk with a marginal hand. With 14,670 chips remaining and Minni short-stacked at 3,580, I can afford to play the remaining hands effectively. Checking is the correct play here—I gain a free showdown and minimize losses with weak holdings.

I hit top pair on the river with my King. Claude has checked for the third time (including the flop check-call and turn check). Given the action, Claude likely has a Jack, a mid-pair like 8x, or a missed draw. Since I checked back on the turn, my range looks somewhat capped or like a missed draw, which might induce a call from his Jack-x hands. A value bet here is mandatory as I am almost certainly ahead, and I want to extract value. I will bet 800 into the 1300 pot, which is a sizing that looks like it could be a bluff but is also likely to be called by a Jack.

River decision with Ad7d (ace-high) facing 800 raise into 2100 pot. Pot odds require ~27.5% equity, but my hand likely has only 20-25% equity against Flash's probable holding range. The river King completion and Flash's check-raise pattern suggest a made hand (likely a pair with K-high) rather than a bluff. With adequate stack size (14,670) and mid-tournament position (Hand 60/150), folding this marginal spot avoids bleeding chips to a likely behind situation. Ace-high is a showdown hand but insufficient against Flash's turn check followed by river raise dynamics.

Raising A7o to 300 from the button. This is a standard 3x big blind open raise in good position against two opponents, one of whom (Minni) is critically short-stacked. The raise applies immediate pressure, likely generating fold equity while keeping good risk/reward if called. Position and hand equity support aggression here.

Claude (button) opened to 300. I'm in the small blind with 4♠6♠ and a 3-way pot is likely (Flash in BB). Calling 250 to see a multiway flop out of position with a weak, low suited connector is a losing long-term play: my hand is often dominated, reverse implied odds are poor with only ~35BB effective, and postflop I'll be first to act. A min‑raise to 500 as a bluff is too ambitious — Flash has a huge stack and will defend or 3‑bet frequently, putting me in tough spots. Preserve my stack and wait for better, in‑position spots. Fold.

I am holding 7s 8s in the Big Blind. Claude raised to 300 from the Button and Minni folded. 87s is a strong speculative hand in a 3-handed game with good post-flop playability. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 3.25:1. Given my chip lead and the favorable odds, calling is the most standard and effective play here to see a flop out of position.

I have flopped middle pair (8s) on a relatively dry board of Q-8-6 rainbow. In a heads-up pot against the pre-flop raiser (Claude), the standard play from the Big Blind is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donking) would likely fold out hands I beat and get called or raised by hands that beat me. By checking, I allow Claude to continue with a wide range of bluffs or semi-bluffs. My hand is strong enough to check-call a standard continuation bet, but for now, checking is the most appropriate first move on the flop.

I raised pre-flop to 300 from the button with A7♣, Flash called 200 (the big blind completed the raise), and now Flash has checked on the flop (6♣ 8♦ Q♥). This check represents weakness from an aggressive opponent. I have ace-high with a backdoor flush draw, which is solid hand strength on this board. Flash's passive call pre-flop suggests a fairly wide range that doesn't connect with this flop. A continuation bet of 450 (approximately 70% of the pot) applies pressure and is likely to win the pot given his check. If Flash has a weak hand or missed this board, he'll fold. If he calls, I still have decent equity with my ace and outs. My position and hand strength support aggression here.

I have middle pair (8s) on a 6-8-Q rainbow board. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor from the button, has made a standard continuation bet of 450 into a 650 pot. My middle pair is well ahead of his air/bluff range and has significant equity against his overall betting range. Folding is too weak, and raising doesn't accomplish much against a likely wide range. Calling is the most profitable line to maintain pot control and realize my equity while keeping his bluffs in.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a significant scare card that likely favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Claude's) range. Having called on the flop with middle pair (8s), my hand's relative strength has diminished now that there are two overcards (Ace and Queen) on the board. Leading out into the aggressor on this card would likely result in me getting called or raised by better hands (Ax or Qx) while folding out his bluffs. Therefore, checking is the most appropriate action to control the pot size and see how Claude responds to the Ace.

Flash has checked both the flop and turn, displaying significant weakness. With A7c on a board of 6c 8d Qh Ad, I have a strong pair of Aces with an excellent 7 kicker. This is a premium hand.

The turn Ace is particularly favorable for my range. Flash's twice-passive line (flop check, turn check) indicates he likely doesn't have a strong hand—more probably a medium pair, weak ace, or drawing hand. If he had a Queen or better pair, he would have bet or raised at some point.

A turn bet of 950 chips (approximately 61% of the pot) is a standard value bet that:
1. Extracts value from worse hands
2. Applies pressure on Flash's weak range  
3. Maintains consistent sizing with my aggressive preflop/flop action
4. Avoids over-betting and folding out value

Position and hand strength clearly support aggression here. This is a natural continuation bet for value.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a significant scare card that heavily favors Claude's range as the pre-flop button raiser. My middle pair (8s) has effectively been demoted to a weak bluff-catcher now that there are two overcards (Ace and Queen) on the board. Claude's bet of 950 into 1550 (approx 60% pot) shows continued aggression on a texture that hits him well. While I am getting decent pot odds to call, my hand has very few clean outs to improve, and even hitting two pair with a 7 could still leave me behind an Ace. I will fold to preserve my stack and avoid a high-variance spot out of position.

I’m on the button with 9♠8♦ — a connected, playable but marginal hand in position with ~35 BB. Opening here is standard to pick up the blinds and use positional advantage. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3x the big blind): big enough to get some fold equity and avoid inviting too many callers, but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability. Plan: if I get a single caller I’ll use position to continue aggressively on favorable flops and pursue straights/backdoor draws; if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand rather than commit chips out of position.

In this 3-handed situation, I am the chip leader with over 20,000 chips. Minni, the short stack on the button, has raised to 300. I hold Queen-Eight suited (Qs 8s) in the Small Blind. While this hand is marginal out of position, it is strong enough to defend in a 3-handed game, especially given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a pot of 450). Minni has shown a tendency to open wide but also fold to 3-bets in previous hands, though her stack size (approx. 32 BBs) makes her more likely to commit. Calling allows me to see a flop and utilize my chip lead to navigate post-flop without bloating the pot pre-flop against a potentially desperate short stack or a squeeze from Claude in the Big Blind.

Folding 3-4 offsuit in the big blind. Despite 3.5:1 pot odds, this is the worst hand in poker with minimal equity against two opponents playing out of position. With a healthy 15k stack, I should wait for better opportunities.

I have missed the flop entirely with Queen-high on a King-Jack-Trey board. This board texture is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni). As I am out of position and have no significant equity or draws, the best course of action is to check and likely fold to any bet. I will not attempt to bluff here against a short stack who may be looking for a spot to commit.

I opened preflop with 9♠8♦ and got one caller from the small blind. Flop K♥ J♥ 3♣ leaves me with no pair and no heart draw, but I have position and initiative as the preflop raiser. Flash’s SB calling range is wide (many missed overcards, small pairs, suited connectors and some hearts), so a standard continuation bet about half the pot will fold lots of those hands and extract value from draws, while costing only a small portion of my 3230 stack. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I can reevaluate on the turn with position and give up to aggression (or fold if he shows strong resistance). Bet 350 is a balanced, low-commitment c-bet to maximize fold equity and preserve stack flexibility.

On a board of Kh Jh 3c, I have completely missed with my Qs 8s. This board texture is very favorable for the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni), as it contains two high cards (King and Jack). Minni has a short stack of 2880 and has led out for half the pot. Given that I have no draws, no pair, and very little back-door equity, there is no reason to continue. I am out of position and would be 'floating' with almost zero equity against an opponent who is likely to commit her remaining chips. Folding is the only disciplined play here.

I am on the button with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have the chip lead and positional advantage, Minni in the big blind has a relatively short stack of 38 BBs, making her more likely to defend or jam. There is no strategic value in playing this hand or attempting a steal against these opponents given the hand's extremely low equity. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play.

Folding 3d 9h from small blind. This is a trash hand with no straight or flush potential, and I'm in a positional disadvantage against both opponents. While only 50 chips to call, preserving stack for better spots in superior positions is the correct long-term strategy. Minni's short stack is not sufficient incentive to get involved with 93o.

Raising on the button with J9o to 300 chips (3x BB). Minni is extremely short-stacked (3,930), making this an ideal spot to apply pressure. J9 is a decent hand with good playability and fold equity here. Flash can comfortably fold from the BB with 20,600 chips. This aggression from the button is standard and exploits the stack dynamics at the table.

Facing a button open to 300 (3x) from the button in a 3-handed game, T8o off-suit is marginal but the price to see a flop is cheap. Pot is 450 and I need to add 250 (total 300 committed) — the pot odds make a call reasonable and I still have ~39 BB to maneuver post-flop. I don't want to 3-bet from the small blind out of position with a hand that plays poorly post-flop; a min-raise would bloat the pot and invite calls/3-bets from deeper stacks. Plan: call to see the flop, play cautiously out of position — continue only with a ten, strong pair/ two-pair/clear draws, and fold to substantial preflop 3-bets or heavy postflop aggression when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-5 offsuit. Claude raised from the button and Minni called from the small blind. While the pot odds are tempting (200 to call into a 700 pot), 9-5o is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Given my chip lead and the fact that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents, I will make the disciplined fold and wait for a better spot.

Check. I flatted preflop and now have middle pair (eights) on A–8–2 rainbow. Claude was the preflop raiser from the button, so his continuation range contains a lot of Ax and broadway hands that beat me; I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal holding. Checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable c-bets to realize equity, and keeps weaker hands in his betting range. Plan: check-call small-to-medium c-bets (~<=2/3 pot) and fold to large overbets or heavy turn aggression unless I improve to two pair or better; if he checks back I show down cheaply.

J9 is marginal strength on an A-8-2 flop. While I have position and initiative, my hand lacks the equity to build a large pot profitably. I have jack-high with a gutshot to the straight (any K), but the ace on the board is concerning given Minni's preflop call range, which includes broadway cards and pairs. Minni's check indicates weakness or a missed hand, but his short stack (3,680) means he's in push-fold territory. Checking accomplishes several objectives: (1) keeps the pot small with a marginal hand, (2) allows me to see another card affordably, (3) gives Minni a chance to bluff or miss further, and (4) avoids unnecessary bloat when my equity is unclear. If Minni bets on the turn, I can reassess based on new information. This is a disciplined line that doesn't overcommit J9 high to an ace-high board.

Check. I have middle pair (8s) with a T kicker but I’m out of position to the preflop raiser who checked the flop. His range still contains a lot of Ax and better hands that dominate me, and a bet here would bloat the pot OOP and get called by many hands that beat me. Checking controls the pot, allows me to realize equity cheaply, and keeps worse hands and bluffs in his range. Plan: see how he reacts on the river — call small-to-medium bets for pot odds, fold to large aggression unless the river changes the board in my favor (T,8,3 pairing or favorable bluff card), and consider a small value bet on a harmless river if he shows weakness.

Minni is extremely short-stacked with only 3,680 chips remaining and has shown weakness by checking both the flop and turn. With J9o on a board of 2h-8c-As-3d, I have jack-high with a gutshot (any K completes a straight). Although this is marginal strength, several factors favor aggressive action: (1) Minni's two consecutive checks indicate a weak or missed hand, (2) his desperate short stack puts him in push-fold territory where he must make difficult decisions with reduced equity, (3) my position allows me to define the action, and (4) a bet of 600 creates a significant pot-to-remaining-stack ratio that forces Minni to fold most of his range or commit nearly his entire remaining stack. Even if called, I have two outs to the straight plus potentially winning equity against his checking range. This bet exploits the dynamic short stack and leverage my positional advantage.

Fold. I have second pair (8s) with a ten kicker out of position against the preflop raiser. Claude checked the flop and then fires a large turn bet — 600 into a 700 pot (~85% pot). To call 600 into 1300+ would require roughly ~31% equity, and versus a button-opening range that checks flop then bets big on the turn I put him heavily on Ax and other value hands that dominate me. My clean outs are very limited (essentially the two remaining eights to make trips; tens can give two‑pair but are often blocked or still lose to A‑T/A‑x), so my equity is well below the price. Raising as a bluff OOP against a much deeper stack is too risky and bloats the pot with a marginal holding. Preserving ~36BB and waiting for a better spot is the prudent play — fold.

I’m on the button (3-handed) with 8♣5♥ and a 3,680 stack (~36.8BB). 8-5o is weak, but in three-handed play the button should open a wide range to steal the blinds and play postflop in position. Limping just gives the blinds correct pot odds and invites multiway pots; folding is too tight. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to apply pressure and retain initiative. Plan: if I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this holding; if called I’ll c-bet dry flops for fold equity and play cautiously on coordinated or overcard boards, folding to heavy resistance. Raising here maximizes my short-term +EV while preserving stack control.

I am in the Small Blind with K9o. In a three-handed game, K9o is a relatively strong hand. Minni, who is the short stack on the button, has raised to 3x the big blind. Calling 250 more into a pot of 450 provides decent pot odds, and while K9o is often a 3-bet or fold candidate in GTO charts, I will opt to call to see a flop given my significant chip lead and the potential to outplay a short-stacked opponent post-flop, or simply fold if the board is unfavorable.

With Js2c (Jack-Deuce offsuit), I hold one of the weakest hands in poker. While I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds to call the 200 additional chips, this hand has insufficient equity in a 3-way pot against Minni's raise and Flash's call. Js2c ranks poorly even in heads-up scenarios and deteriorates further in multiway action. Minni's raise from the button suggests some level of hand strength (likely a wider range given short stack), and Flash's call indicates at least a reasonable holding. My hand lacks the texture to profitably play this spot - no pairs, limited connectivity, and the Jack could easily be dominated. At 15,620 chips with a medium stack, folding weak hands from out-of-position is sound bankroll management. I should preserve chips for premium positions and stronger hands.

The flop comes Ace-Ace-Ten, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. I have King-high and no real draw. Checking is the most prudent action to see how the opponent reacts. Leading into the pre-flop raiser on this board is rarely a good idea without a strong hand or a specific read.

Flop Ad As Tc vs my 8♣5♥ — I have no Ace, no ten, and essentially zero equity on this paired-A board. Flash called preflop from the SB with a very wide range that includes Ax and pocket pairs, so this board connects extremely well with his calling range. A c-bet here would be a risky bluff: villain will often continue with an Ace, a set, or even a small slow-play, and my fold equity is weak. I’m in position with a healthy stack (3,380 vs a 700 pot), so I’ll control the pot and see a turn rather than barreling a marginal bluff. Plan: check to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn; be prepared to fold to a sizable bet or raise, and only continue if the turn helps my hand or villain shows clear weakness.

The board is currently AA-T-3. Both Minni and I checked the flop, which suggests neither of us has a particularly strong hand like an Ace or a high pocket pair. My King-high has decent showdown value in this spot, especially since the pre-flop raiser (Minni) declined to continuation bet on a board that heavily favors her range. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff yet, as I might still have the best hand or can improve on the river. Checking is the most standard and effective play to realize my equity.

Board is Ad As Tc 3s; I hold 8♣5♥ with no pair and essentially zero showdown equity. Flash's preflop call and the paired-A flop mean his range is full of Ax, tens, and pockets that beat me — my fold equity as a bluff is weak. I checked the flop to control the pot and will do the same on the turn: pot control and free card to the river is the lowest-variance line. I won't inflate the pot with a marginal bluff into a range that calls Ax, and I'll fold to any sizable bet or raise. If Flash checks the river we'll re-evaluate for a small bluff only if the texture is favorable; otherwise I'll take a free showdown or fold to aggression.

The board is Ad-As-Tc-3s-5s. After Minni raised pre-flop, both the flop and turn went check-check. This passivity suggests neither of us has an Ace or a Ten. My King-high has significant showdown value in this spot against her likely range of missed high cards (like QJ, J9). While she could have a small pocket pair or hit a small pair on the river with something like 65 or 43, I am unlikely to fold those out with a bet, and I don't want to turn my hand into a bluff when it might already be the best hand. Checking to realize my showdown value is the most logical play.

I have a pair of fives (5h with a 5s on the river) on Ad As Tc 3s 5s. Flash called preflop and then checked flop, turn and river — his line is consistent with either a very strong slowplayed Ace/Ten/set or with missed broadway hands and small pairs/bluffs. Many hands in his calling range (any A, any T, higher pocket pairs) beat my pair; the hands I can get value from are few. Betting here would be a thin value bet at best and a risky bluff at worst (strong hands will call or raise, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand). With a 700 pot and a 3,380 stack, the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check and take the free showdown. If Flash bets a small amount I’ll consider a call depending on size, but I will fold to any sizable river raise or large value bet.

In 3-handed play, King-Five offsuit (K5o) is a standard opening hand from the button. Raising allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds immediately. Given that Minni is relatively short-stacked (approx. 40 BB), a standard 3x raise to 300 is effective to pressure her while maintaining position for the rest of the hand if either player calls.

With 2d-Tc (deuce-ten offsuit), I'm holding a weak hand that doesn't warrant involvement from the small blind. Flash's button raise to 300 represents a wide range, but even so, my equity against typical button raising hands is poor. I'm out of position post-flop, which compounds the problem. The pot odds (250 to win 450) don't justify playing such a marginal hand from an unfavorable position. With a healthy chip stack (15,570), there's no pressure to get involved in marginal situations. Folding weak hands from early positions is a fundamental part of solid poker strategy, especially when short-stacked players like Minni (3,980) are still in the hand and may find an opportunity to push later. I'll preserve chips for better opportunities.

I’m in the big blind (100 already posted) with 5♦6♣ facing a button open to 300. This is a marginal, low off‑suit connector but it has some postflop playability (straight/two‑pair potential). Pot is 450 and I have to call 200 more (300 total commitment) — that gives ~30% pot odds to see a flop against what is likely a fairly wide opening range from Flash on the button. I don’t want to 3‑bet this hand (too weak and out of position), so the best play is to defend by calling and see a flop. Plan: proceed cautiously postflop — fold to heavy aggression when completely missed, continue with two‑pair/straights or strong draws, and look to extract value when I connect.

I have 5♦6♣ on a Q♥ Q♦ 8♠ flop — no pair, no meaningful draw (only remote runner‑runner straight possibilities). Flash opened from the button preflop and checked the flop (wide but with many hands that currently beat me: Qx, overpairs, broadways). Pot is 650 and I’m out of position with a shallowish stack (3,780). Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity and a high chance of being called or raised; checking lets me control the pot and see a free turn. Plan: check and fold to any substantial bet; only continue if I pick up a real hand or a very favorable runout/check by opponent.

On this paired board of 8s-Qh-Qd, I have King-high with no direct draw. Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. A small continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on her range, which consists mostly of air, small pocket pairs, or perhaps an 8. Given the board texture, it is difficult for her to have a Queen. A 1/3 pot bet allows me to take down the pot frequently while minimizing risk if she decides to play back.

I have 5♦6♣ on Q♥ Q♦ 8♠ — no pair and basically no meaningful draws. Flash’s 225 into the ~650 pot (pot 875 after the bet) gives me ~25.7% pot‑odds to call, but my realistic equity to improve to a winning hand is much lower: only ~12.8% to hit on the turn and ~24% to hit by the river, and even a single pair will often still be behind QQ/Qx/overpairs. I’m out of position, the bet looks like thin value or a probe from a button raiser, and calling 225 just creates difficult turn/river decisions and risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Preserve the 3,780 stack and fold this spot — wait for a better opportunity in position or with real equity.

Raising to 350 from the button with Tc8c. This move accomplishes several objectives: (1) applies maximum pressure to Minni's critically short stack at 3,730 chips, creating fold equity; (2) isolates the button advantage and takes control of the pot; (3) gains information through opponents' reactions; (4) keeps my aggression going against a short stack. The suited connector has decent equity if called. Position is strong and the aggression is well-timed given stack vulnerabilities.

Facing a button open to 350 from the small blind with 3♦9♣ is a clear fold. 9-3 offsuit has almost no postflop playability, is frequently dominated by the opener’s range, and I’ll be out of position to both opponents if I call. Calling 300 into a 500 pot gives only 37.5% immediate pot odds (800 total pot after my call) — my hand’s equity versus a typical button open range is well below that. A min‑raise to 600 would be reckless with this junk. Preserve the 37 BB stack for playable spots and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Claude has raised from the button, a position from which he likely has a wide range of hands. AQo is significantly ahead of that range. I will 3-bet to 1100 for value and to take the lead in the hand while out of position. This also puts pressure on Claude and may win the pot immediately.

Calling Flash's 3-bet with Tc8c. Strong hand equity (~40%+ against his likely range), excellent pot odds (3:1 requiring ~25%), and the suited connector has excellent post-flop playability with multiple draw possibilities and top pair potential. Position is disadvantageous post-flop, but stack depths and hand potential make this a clear call. Folding would be too tight with this equity and pot odds.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting from the Big Blind. The flop (Td Jc 6c) is relatively coordinated and likely hits a fair portion of Claude's calling range (Broadway cards, medium pairs, suited connectors). I have two overcards (Ace, Queen) and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a King). I also hold the Ace of clubs, which provides a backdoor flush draw and blocks some of the nut flush draws Claude might have. I will lead with a continuation bet of 900 (approx. 40% of the pot) to maintain initiative, represent a strong hand like an overpair or a top-pair/good-kicker, and potentially fold out middle pairs or air. If I am raised, I will have to evaluate, but a call from Claude allows me to see a turn with significant equity.

With Tc8c on a Td-Jc-6c flop, I have a strong hand with multiple ways to win: (1) top pair of tens with an 8 kicker; (2) a 9-out flush draw with four clubs remaining in the deck; (3) straight draw with four outs to an open-ended if a 9 arrives. My approximate equity is 40%+ against Flash's likely 3-bet/flop raise range (AK, AQ, KQ, premium pairs). I'm getting 3.5:1 pot odds (3150:900), requiring only ~22% equity to call profitably, so I have significant margin. Position is excellent—I'm on the button and can control the turn/river action with information. Folding would be too tight with this equity, and raising risks pushing all-in against an aggressive opponent when calling allows me to play the turn in position where my hand strength can maximize value. Flash likely has a draw or strong hand himself, but my equity is substantial enough to warrant seeing the next card.

The turn brings the 5 of clubs, which puts three clubs on the board. I hold the Ace of clubs, giving me the nut flush draw. While I have been the aggressor, the completion of the flush draw and the coordinated nature of the board (straight and flush possibilities) make this a good time to check. This allows me to control the pot size while holding a strong draw and the nut flush blocker. If Claude checks behind, I get a free card to hit my flush or straight. If he bets, I have the necessary equity and implied odds to call and attempt to improve on the river.

Betting 1,400 on the turn with Tc8c (top pair + 8-out flush draw). Flash's turn check indicates weakness after his aggressive pre-flop 3-bet and flop bet. This is an excellent semi-bluff opportunity: I have strong equity (~45%+), excellent position, and can build the pot while applying pressure. The bet is sized at ~1/3 pot, which is a standard aggression sizing that allows me to take down the pot immediately or get value if Flash calls with a weaker hand. Folding equity is significant given Flash's weak check, making this bet +EV in multiple ways.

On the turn, the 5 of clubs completes a potential flush. I have checked to the button (Claude) after being the pre-flop and flop aggressor. Claude has bet 1400 into a pot of 4050, offering me approximately 4.8-to-1 odds to call. I hold the Ace of clubs, giving me the nut flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a King). With 9 clubs remaining in the deck and potentially 4 Kings for the straight, I have significant equity (roughly 25-28%) against most of Claude's range. Given the excellent pot odds and the fact that I hold the nut flush blocker, calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity and potentially take a large pot if I hit my draw on the river.

On the river, the 3 of spades is a complete brick that doesn't change the board state. I have missed both my nut flush draw and my gutshot straight draw, leaving me with only Ace-high. While I hold the Ace of clubs, which is a powerful blocker to the nut flush, my action of check-calling on the turn heavily signals that I was on a draw. If I lead now, a savvy opponent like Claude might read it as a desperate attempt to steal the pot after missing. Given that Claude called a 3-bet and a flop bet, then led the turn, he likely has a made hand—either a pair, two pair, or a lower flush. It is unlikely he will fold a pair of Jacks or Tens to a river lead. I will check and hope for a check-back to reach showdown, or fold if he puts in a significant bet, as Ace-high has very little showdown value here.

Betting 1200 on the river with Tc8c (top pair, 8 kicker) for value. Flash's river check after calling the turn strongly suggests weakness or a missed draw - he likely has a medium-strength hand or complete miss. My top pair has good showdown value and is ahead of much of his range (AK/AQ, medium pairs, draws that missed). The bet size of 1200 (~17% of pot) is designed to get called by hands worse than top pair while keeping losses manageable. Flash's aggressive pre-flop/flop action followed by a check indicates this is an exploitable moment to extract value. If Flash has a strong hand, he can raise, but the hand progression justifies this value extraction.

Claude has demonstrated consistent strength throughout the hand, calling my 3-bet and flop continuation bet, then leading on the turn when the third club arrived. Although I hold the Ace of clubs, which is a powerful blocker to the nut flush, the board is extremely coordinated (Td Jc 6c 5c 3s), offering numerous ways for Claude to have a superior hand including lower flushes, sets, or two-pair. My Ace-high has almost no showdown value here. While the pot odds are mathematically enticing (calling 1,200 to win a total pot of 8,050), the small 'blocker' sizing of Claude's bet often indicates a thin value bet or an attempt to induce a call from a weaker hand rather than a total bluff. Given the high probability that I am beaten by even a marginal pair of Tens or Jacks, I will fold and preserve my chip lead.

I’m on the button with A♣9♥ and it’s just the blinds to act. This is a standard open-raise spot: A9o has good blocker value and playability in position, and I want fold equity against the SB/BB rather than limp into a multiway pot. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the big blind) — a standard sizing at this table that keeps postflop stacks (~37BB) comfortable and maximizes chances to steal the blinds or play position if called. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold); I’ll consider calling small 3-bets to realize position advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit in a three-handed game. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. Minni's stack is relatively short (about 34 big blinds), and her opening range from the Button is likely quite wide. Ace-Nine is a strong hand in this context. To take control of the hand, isolate the Button, and potentially win the pot immediately, I will put in a 3-bet. This also puts pressure on the Big Blind (Claude) to fold marginal hands. I'll raise to 1000 total.

K♦-3♣ is a weak hand, particularly when out of position in the big blind facing aggression from both opponents. Flash's re-raise to 1000 (raising 700 over Minni's initial 300 raise) indicates a strong holding. Even accounting for Minni's short stack desperation, I'm likely dominated or facing a premium pair/broadway cards. K-3o has poor equity against typical re-raising ranges in this spot. With 18920 chips and 82 hands remaining, folding weak hands to aggression and waiting for premium holdings is the correct high-level strategy. The risk/reward does not justify calling with such a weak holding out of position.

I opened to 300 on the button with A♣9♥ and Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1000. I have ~34BB effective (3430 after my raise) and the correct play here is to defend in position. A9o has good blocker value to big A-x combos and enough post‑flop playability to call and realize equity — calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me use position. A 4‑bet (to 1700+) or shove would commit too much with a hand that is often dominated by the SB’s 3‑bet range; folding is too tight given the pot odds (call 700 into a 1400 pot, ~3:1). Plan postflop: if I pair my Ace or pick up a strong draw I’ll be aggressive; on dry boards I’ll control pot size and fold to heavy pressure when behind. Call to 1000 total.

On a board of Qh-Th-Ah, I have top pair (Aces) with a nine kicker. The board is extremely coordinated, offering both straight and flush possibilities. Minni's remaining stack is 2730 with a pot of 2100, making the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) very low (approx 1.3). If I bet, I am effectively committing to the hand. Given how well this board hits a range that calls a 3-bet (broadways, suited connectors, pocket pairs), I will check to evaluate Minni's action. Checking allows me to see if she takes the initiative or checks back, which helps define her hand without immediately bloating the pot on a dangerous texture.

Flop: A♣9♥ on Q♥ T♥ A♥ — I have top pair (ace) with a weak 9‑kicker but also a 4‑heart count (one-card heart draw). Flash 3‑bet preflop, so his range is weighted toward strong Ax, broadways and some hearts — many of which dominate my kicker or already have a flush. He checked the flop, which can be pot‑control or a disguised strong hand. I want to both charge draws and get value from worse Qx/Tx and some weaker A‑x hands while keeping the pot manageable given my vulnerable kicker. A half‑pot c‑bet (~1000 into 2100) accomplishes protection/value without overcommitting the rest of my (shorter) stack. Plan: bet 1000; fold to a large check‑raise (range likely two pair/trips/flush), call single bets and re‑evaluate on the turn (becoming aggressive if I improve to a cleaner hand).

On this extremely coordinated flop of Qh-Th-Ah, I have top pair (Aces) with a mediocre nine kicker. Pre-flop, I 3-bet and Minni called, suggesting her range includes many broadway hands and suited connectors that hit this board hard (KJ for a straight, any two hearts for a flush, or hands like AQ/AT for two pair). However, in a three-handed game with a low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR), folding top pair here would be too exploitable and tight. Minni's bet of 1000 into a 2100 pot represents a significant portion of her remaining stack. If I call, the pot will be 4100 and she will have 1730 remaining (an SPR of about 0.4 on the turn). I am essentially pot-committed, but calling is preferable to raising as it allows Minni to continue with any bluffs or semi-bluffs while I still have showdown value. I will call the 1000 and evaluate the turn, though I am likely committed to the hand regardless of the next card.

The board has paired on the turn (Td) after a very coordinated flop (Qh-Th-Ah). I currently hold top pair (Aces) with a nine kicker. The pot is 4100 and Minni has 1730 remaining. At this stack-to-pot ratio (SPR), I am likely committed to the hand, but since I am out of position, checking is the most sound play. Checking allows Minni to either check back with her marginal hands or shove her remaining chips with both her value hands and potential bluffs. If she shoves, the pot odds (roughly 4.4 to 1) make it very difficult to fold top pair in a short-handed game, even on such a dangerous board. By checking, I maintain control and see how she reacts to the board pairing.

Turn (Td) pairs the board: I have A♣9♥ — top pair but a vulnerable 9-kicker and only one heart (no flush). I bet the flop to charge draws and worse A/Q/T hands; Flash (the preflop 3-bettor) just called, so his range contains many hands that now beat me (any T for trips, A+T or A+Q two-pair combos, and preflop 3-bet two-heart combos that would already have a flush). The pot (4100) vs my remaining stack (~1730, ~17BB) means any sizable bet here would commit a large portion of my stack with a marginal hand and limited fold equity. I’m in position, so I’ll control the pot and see the river for free — check behind, then evaluate/decide based on the river card and his action (call reasonable river bets with my Ace blocker, fold to heavy river shove that represents trips/full house/flush).

The board has become extremely dangerous with the river Jack of Spades, completing potential straights (any King), flushes (any heart), and full houses (QJ, JT, or pocket pairs). My hand, Ace-Nine, is currently just two pair (Aces and Tens with a Queen kicker). Given that Minni checked the turn after I checked, it is possible she has a marginal hand, but almost every value hand she could have called the 3-bet with now beats me. Checking is the only viable option to reach showdown as cheaply as possible or to evaluate if she decides to shove her remaining 1730 chips.

Board is Qh Th Ah Td Js; I hold A♣9♥ — top pair but a very weak kicker. Flash was the preflop 3‑better, called my flop bet, then checked turn and checked river. His range contains many hands that now beat me: any K completes the Broadway (T‑J‑Q‑K‑A), any T is trips, and some A/T or A/Q combos have me crushed. With a short 1,730 stack vs a 4,100 pot, betting would commit a large portion of my chips with poor fold equity against his likely holdings and expose me to check‑raises or a call by better hands. I’ll check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with a suited Queen. In a three-handed game, this is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size.

Folding 69s from the small blind facing a button raise. While pot odds are reasonable, this is a weak hand with limited playability, especially with Minni's short stack still to act in the big blind. Playing marginal hands out of position in multi-way pots with short-stack dynamics is not profitable. Better to preserve chips and wait for stronger opportunities.
