Facing a button raise to 300 with 6♦4♣ in the big blind. The raise is small (pot 450, I need to call 200 more = commit 300 total), so the pot odds are roughly 30% and the call is only ~5% of my stack (3680). 6-4 offsuit is a weak hand, but against a likely wide button opening range it’s defendable at this price. 3-betting OOP with this hand is a poor play — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with garbage. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed only with real equity or a favorable board; fold to significant 3-bets/large shove or give up vs heavy turn aggression when missed.

Flop is 7♥ K♦ 2♣ and I hold 6♦4♣ — no pair and no meaningful draws (only very remote backdoor possibilities). I called preflop as a cheap defense to a wide button open, but I’ve completely missed this dry K-high board. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially zero equity. Check to control the pot, see if the button continuation-bets, and fold to standard c-bets (or only continue vs a very small bet or if I pick up real equity on the turn). If the aggressor checks back I get a free card to reassess.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the board (K-7-2 rainbow) is very dry, favoring my perceived range. Minni checked to me, and a standard continuation bet should be enough to take down the pot most of the time. I will bet 300 into the 650 pot.

Flop: 7♥ K♦ 2♣ — I have 6♦4♣ (no pair, no real draw). Flash bets 300 into a 950 pot. Calling 300 gives me ~24% pot odds (300/(950+300) ≈ 24%). My hand has about 6 outs to pair by the river (≈24% to pair by river), so this is a marginal but defensible call given the small bet and that Flash likely has a wide c-betting range. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to build a big pot — plan is to call one street, see the turn, and fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I improve (pair or meaningful draw). If I pick up a pair or a backdoor improvement I can proceed; otherwise I’ll release to pressure on the turn. Stack cost is small (~8–9% of my stack), so I’ll call and reevaluate on the turn.

I hold 6♦4♣ on 7♥ K♦ 2♣ 8♦ — I have no pair and no real draw (I can only hit three-of-a-kind with a 6 or 4 or a 5 to make a 4-5-6-7-8 straight). That’s roughly ~7 outs (~15% to improve on the river), so my equity is low. Pot is 1,250 and I’m out of position; there’s no reason to build the pot with trash. I’ll check to control the pot, concede to big aggression, call only small turn bets for correct pot odds, and re-evaluate on the river (if Flash checks back I get a free card; if I pair or get a clear draw on the river I can proceed).

I have picked up a diamond flush draw on the turn to go with my Queen high. Since Minni called my flop continuation bet, she likely has a piece of this board, such as a 7 or a King. Checking back here allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if a diamond or a Queen hits. It also prevents me from being forced into a difficult decision if the short-stacked Minni decides to check-raise or shove over a second barrel.

Board: 7♥ K♦ 2♣ 8♦ Q♠. I hold 6♦4♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Pot is 1,250 and my stack is 3,180. Flash opened preflop and c-bet the flop (wide range), then checked the turn — that check looks like pot control or weakness. The queen on the river improves many hands in his range (Qx, some Kx, pocket pairs) and he can comfortably call small bluffs given his deep stack. I have almost no showdown equity and very limited fold equity: a small bet will get called too often; shoving is high-variance and unnecessary with my short stack relative to tournament life. Given I missed and there's no compelling reason to bloat the pot, the prudent play is to check and take the showdown (or fold if he acts — but he hasn't). Check to preserve chips and pick better spots to bluff or get value.

I have improved to a pair of Queens on the river. After Minni called my flop bet and checked both the turn and the river, I have significant showdown value. While she could be trapping with a King, her range also includes 7x, 8x, or pocket pairs that I now beat. Betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and might only provoke a fold or a call from a better hand (like a King). Therefore, checking back to realize my equity and take the pot at showdown is the most consistent and safe play.

I'm holding 5d3d on the button, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker. Despite having positional advantage as the button, hand quality is paramount. This combination offers no flush potential, no straight potential, and very weak card values. With a healthy stack of 18,870, there's no reason to get involved with this hand. I can afford to be selective and wait for premium or playable holdings. Folding is the mathematically correct decision here - even with position, 53o cannot be profitably played against opponents who raise or call.

Heads-up in the small blind with A♥2♠ and ~31BB effective. This is a standard open-raise spot — A2o plays well heads-up and I want to seize the initiative rather than limp and play OOP passively. The pot is 150 and calling 50 gives the BB a cheap look; raising to 200 (2x the BB) creates fold equity, narrows the BB’s range, and still leaves plenty of stack depth for postflop play. I’ll make a standard min/open to 200 to take control of the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Minni) has min-raised to 200. Minni has a short stack of about 30 big blinds. I have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. With pot odds of 3:1 (100 to call into a 300 pot) and a hand that performs well in blind-vs-blind scenarios, calling is a very profitable play. I want to keep Minni's range wide and see a flop where I can utilize my positional advantage.

I opened preflop to take the initiative heads‑up with A♥2♠. Flop 6♦6♠8♥ is dry but paired — there are no meaningful flush draws and my hand is just Ace‑high with ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (~24% to improve). The BB’s calling range here contains a lot of unmade hands (broadways, Kx/Qx, some small pairs) but also hands that beat me (6x trips, any 8x, some pocket pairs). As the preflop aggressor I should continuation‑bet a wide range to fold out missed hands and extract value from worse. I’ll make a relatively small c‑bet of 150 (~37.5% of the pot) — big enough to apply pressure and fold some of his weaker holdings, but small enough to keep the pot controllable with a marginal Ace‑high. If he folds I take it down; if he calls I can realize equity and re‑evaluate the turn (bet again if I pair or if the turn improves my range, pot‑control or check back otherwise). If he check‑raises heavily (a pot‑sized raise or a large overbet) I’ll assume strong value (trips/8x) and fold.

On a paired flop of 6-6-8, Minni has led out with a small bet of 150 into 400. I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) which have decent equity against a wide Small Blind opening range. The pot odds are favorable (nearly 3.7:1), and I have the advantage of position. Raising right now might force her to fold air that I currently beat or get me committed against a better hand. By calling, I can see how the turn develops and potentially win the pot if she checks or if I improve to a pair.

I c‑bet the dry 6♦6♠8♥ flop with A♥2♠ and got called. The turn 7♣ doesn’t improve me — I only have ~6 clean outs (three Aces, three Deuces) ≈13% to pair on the river. Pot is 700 and there’s no bet to face. Betting now would bloat the pot and will usually be called by hands that already beat me (8x, any pocket pair that called the flop, or a turned 7), while my fold equity is limited against this deep‑stacked caller. I prefer pot control and to realize my equity — check and evaluate the river. If Villain fires a reasonable bet on the turn I can fold; if he checks back I get a free card to improve.

On a board of 6-6-8-7, the Small Blind (Minni) has checked to me after leading on the flop. My hand, Queen-Ten high, currently has no made value but does have two overcards that could give me the best hand on the river. By checking back, I realize my equity for free and avoid being check-raised by a hand like a six or a pocket pair that Minni might be playing cautiously. If Minni is bluffing or has a weak draw, I might still have the best hand at showdown or can evaluate the river based on her next action.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry 6♣6♠8♥ flop to fold out misses and realize equity. Villain called the small flop c‑bet and then checked turn and river — a very passive line that can represent either medium/weak one‑pair hands, slowplays, or wide missed broadway hands. I have nothing at showdown (A♠2♠ missed), and my only way to win is a pure bluff. To make a half‑pot bluff (~350) profitable Villain needs to fold ~33% of the time; given he called the flop and then checked down, I expect his calling frequency to be higher than that. Betting here risks a lot of chips with very thin fold equity against a player who looks sticky. With ~28BB and a 700 pot, pot‑control and preserving stack equity is the better play — check and show down (or fold to any river bet).

On a final board of 6-6-8-7-3, Minni has checked twice (turn and river) after leading on the flop. This line typically indicates a weak showdown hand like Ace-high or a small pocket pair that is looking to get to a cheap showdown. My Queen-high has very little showdown value, but it is unlikely that a bluff will succeed given her short stack and the likelihood that she holds a hand like Ace-high which she may not fold. Checking back allows me to see the showdown without risking further chips against a range that likely beats me.

Fold. 2♦7♠ is the worst starting hand (very little preflop equity and terrible postflop playability). Although I'm on the button, calling 100 to play a multi-way pot with this holding is low EV and opens up difficult postflop decisions against two deep-stacked opponents who can easily defend or 3‑bet. A light raise to 200 to try and steal is an option sometimes, but given the deeper stacks and no clear weakness from the blinds, that's unnecessarily risky. Preserve my ~35BB stack and wait for a much better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. The Button (Minni) has folded. This is a blind-versus-blind situation against Claude, who is the current chip leader. J3o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, the expected value of playing this hand against a capable opponent like Claude is negative. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with King-high. While K3o is a relatively weak hand, opening from the button is a standard play to exert pressure on the blinds, especially with Minni being a short stack at roughly 34 big blinds. This raise serves as a steal attempt, and I can comfortably fold if the short stack decides to shove or if Claude 3-bets from the small blind. K-high often holds up well post-flop in short-handed play if I get called.

Calling with As-4s from small blind. Getting 1.8:1 pot odds (450 to call 250) is too good to pass up. While A4o is a weak ace, the cheap entry price and potential to hit allows me to realize my equity. Out of position is a concern, but I can fold weak flops. Against Flash's button raising range, I have reasonable equity.

I am in the big blind with K♣6♣ facing a BTN open to 300 and the SB called. Pot is 700 and it costs me 200 more to call (300 total). I have ~34bb effective — K6s is playable but marginal and often dominated by opponents' raising ranges, especially multiway. A small 3-bet to 500 would bloat the pot with a weak hand and risk committing chips out of position; folding is too tight given the cheap price. Calling 300 total preserves stack depth, realizes equity (flush/backdoor possibilities and potential top-pair with a king) and allows me to evaluate the flop. Postflop I’ll proceed cautiously: continue with strong pair/draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards where I'm behind.

With A4s on a Jc-6h-5c flop, I'm holding ace-high with a backdoor flush draw - a marginal hand that's unlikely to be ahead of Flash's button-raising range or Minni's big blind call. I'm out of position and need to be cautious. Checking allows me to control the pot size, gather information about the strength of my opponents' hands, and avoid building a pot with weak equity. If I check, I can potentially get to the turn cheaply and reassess if my ace holds up or if I hit additional outs. A lead bet here lacks credibility given the board texture and my hand strength.

Board is J♣ 6♥ 5♣ and I hold K♣6♣ — I have second pair (6s) with a K kicker plus a very strong club draw (4 clubs known, 9 club outs). Against the BTN’s wide raising range and an SB caller this is a strong semi-bluff/value combo: good raw equity and fold equity. Pot is 900 and both checked to me, so I want a sized c-bet that (a) charges overcards/floaters and weaker draws, (b) denies free cards, and (c) keeps commitment reasonable with ~34bb effective stacks. I’ll bet 300 (~1/3 pot) — small enough to get called by worse and to maintain flexibility, but large enough to take the pot now sometimes. If I get a single raise I will strongly consider calling to realize my equity (I have ~9 clubs + extra pair/trip outs — roughly 14 distinct ways to improve and ~50% to improve by river vs one hand); if I face heavy aggression or a shove I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent, folding to extreme overbets but calling modest pressure to see a turn.

On a J-6-5 flop with two clubs, my King-high has very little equity. Minni, the short stack, led out with a 1/3 pot bet into two opponents. While this could be a defensive bet or a draw, I have no piece of the board and Claude is still to act behind me. Calling with just King-high is too loose given the board texture and the presence of the short stack who may be looking for a double-up. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Calling 300 into a 1200-chip pot. With As4s on Jc-6h-5c, I'm holding ace-high with backdoor flush draw and potential wheel straight draws (need 2 or 3). Against Minni's short-stacked raise from the big blind (he only has ~2930 behind), his range is quite wide - could include any pair, two pair, or even a semi-bluff with a draw. I'm getting roughly 1500 to 300 odds (5:1), needing only ~17% equity to call profitably. My hand likely has 25-35% equity here depending on Minni's exact holdings. This is a heads-up situation now that Flash folded. I can control the hand on the turn and fold to further aggression if I don't improve or pick up additional outs. The bet sizing and Minni's stack size suggest he could be leveraging his short position, so my draw equity is valuable here.

Minni checked the turn after his flop aggression, indicating a pot-control move. With As-4s on Jc-6h-5c-8h, I'm holding ace-high with ~12 outs (backdoor flush draw + wheel outs). My hand is likely trailing his range but has reasonable equity to the river. Checking is the correct play: it prevents me from building a pot with a weak hand out of position, allows me to see the river cheaply to potentially improve, and maintains flexibility for the final street decision. If I brick the river and Minni bets, I can fold. If I hit my draw, I can play accordingly.

Turn decision: board J♣ 6♥ 5♣ 8♥; I hold K♣6♣ — second pair (6s) with a very strong club draw. That’s 9 club outs + the two remaining sixes = 11 clean outs to improve on the river (~11/46 ≈ 24%). Claude called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn, which looks like pot control with a wide calling/checking range (Jx, small pocket pairs, some clubs/draws, or marginal hands). Betting now would rarely get folds from Jx or pocket pairs and would inflate a pot I don’t want to commit with just second pair. Checking controls the pot, realizes my respectable equity, keeps worse hands and bluffs in so I can extract value on the river when I improve (or possibly induce bluffs), and avoids being forced off by a large turn raise. Plan: check and evaluate the river — call reasonable river bets to realize my ~24% to improve; if checked to me on the river I’ll size a value bet if I’ve improved or use a fold/raise depending on the runout and opponent’s river action.

River check with ace-high. I have no showdown value against Minni's range after he raised the flop and checked both turn and river. Betting accomplishes nothing - I can't win via value or bluff. Going to showdown minimizes losses with a hand that's almost certainly beaten. Minni's short stack means he likely has something but checked the river, suggesting a medium-strength hand or complete missed draw. Checking is the only logical option.

Board: J♣ 6♥ 5♣ 8♥ J♥. I hold K♣6♣ — only second pair (sixes) with a K kicker; my club flush missed. Claude called my 300 flop c-bet, then checked turn and checked the river when the J paired; that line looks like a medium-strength calling/controlling range (small pairs, 6x, some floats/missed draws) or a deceptive slow-play, but it also allows for hands that now beat me (any J) and hands I beat. A river bet would mostly fold out air and get called by better hands, and a check-raise would put me in a difficult, often losing, spot. Given the paired board and my marginal showdown value, I’ll check and realize equity rather than inflate the pot with a one-pair hand.

With 7h-Th on the button, I have a decent suited connector that plays well in position. The pot is 150 with 100 to call. Minni is critically short-stacked at only 4,380 chips (44 BBs), making them likely in push/fold mode. Flash has 17,150 chips and is relatively aggressive.

7-10 of hearts has good playability with straight and flush potential. Being on the button gives me position advantage post-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is the correct play here because it:

1. Shows strength and seizes the initiative
2. Allows me to play in position post-flop if called
3. Builds the pot with a hand that can improve
4. Is not so aggressive that it unnecessarily induces all-in situations against the short stack if I want to avoid them
5. Maintains good stack management while taking profitable spots

This is a standard raise from the button with a playable hand in chip position and stack depth.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with K♠4♣: this is an out-of-position, offsuit, heavily dominated hand with very poor postflop playability. Calling 250 is cheap (~36% pot odds) but I’ll be OOP against the opener (and still have the BB to act), so I’m unlikely to realize equity or extract value. A min-raise to 500 would be a weak, speculative 3-bet with a bad hand. Preserve my ~43.8BB stack and fold to the raise.

I am holding As 2s in the Big Blind. Claude has raised from the Button, which is a standard steal attempt or value raise with a wide range in a 3-handed game. Suited Aces are strong hands that play well post-flop. While a 3-bet is a viable aggressive option to take the initiative, calling is a solid, lower-variance play that allows me to defend my blind and see a flop with a hand that has nut potential. I will call the additional 200 chips.

I am first to act on the flop. The board is Kd 7c 3h, which favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Claude), as it contains many high cards and potential pocket pairs. I hold Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. In this situation, leading (donking) is generally not advisable as it gives away information and allows the opponent to play perfectly. Checking is the standard play here to see how Claude responds to the K-high board, allowing me to potentially realize my equity or fold if the aggression is too high.

Betting for value with top pair (7s) and strong kicker (T). The K-7-3 board is dry, and Flash's check indicates weakness. I also have multiple outs (OESD and flush draw). A bet of 300 builds the pot, tests Flash's commitment, and extracts value from worse holdings. This is a standard continuation bet that maintains my aggressive betting line from pre-flop.

I am holding Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw. Claude's bet of 300 into a 650 pot is a standard continuation bet on a King-high board that favors his pre-flop raising range. However, in a 3-handed game, Ace-high still has significant equity against a wide button-opening range. I am getting roughly 3.1:1 on a call, and I have the potential to improve to the best hand on an Ace or pick up significant equity on a spade turn. Folding immediately to a single c-bet with a suited Ace would be overly tight, so I will call to see a turn card.

I am first to act on the turn with the board showing 7c Kd 3h Td. My hand is currently Ace-high (As 2s). The Ten of Diamonds is a card that could potentially improve Claude's button opening range (hands like AT, KT, or JT). Since I called his continuation bet on the flop, my range looks like it has some connection to the board or a drawing hand. Leading out here (donk betting) would likely be met with a fold from his air and a call/raise from his value hands, which isn't ideal for my hand. Checking is the best play to see if Claude checks back for pot control or continues his aggression. If he checks back, I can see a free river and potentially win with Ace-high or improve to a pair of Aces.

Betting 750 on the turn with two pair (7s and 10s). Flash has checked both the flop and turn, indicating weakness. This bet:
1. Extracts value from worse hands and marginal holdings
2. Gets Flash to commit more chips when I'm likely ahead
3. Maintains good pot control and sizing logic
4. Is appropriately sized (60% of pot) to build value while keeping Flash in the hand
5. Represents a natural continuation of my line from the flop bet

With two pair and Flash's passive play, this is a value bet that maximizes my profitability on this turn.

I am currently holding Ace-high (As 2s) on a board of 7c-Kd-3h-Td. I previously called a continuation bet on the flop thinking my Ace-high might have some equity or that the opponent was potentially bluffing a dry board. However, the Ten on the turn is a significant card that connects well with a Button-opening range (hands like AT, KT, QT, JT). Claude is firing a second barrel of 750 into a 1250 pot (60% pot), showing consistent aggression. Given that I have no draw and only three potentially 'clean' outs to an Ace (which could still be behind hands like AK or AT), calling here is no longer mathematically or strategically sound. I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

3-handed table and I’m on the button with 43.8 BB — I should be opening a fairly wide range and take advantage of position postflop. T♠4♦ is marginal but acceptable as part of a button opening range; a standard 3x open to 300 applies pressure to the blinds and keeps the pot size manageable if called. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this hand; if I get callers I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops (and pot-control or check-fold on dry, unpromising boards). Raising to 300 preserves my image, gives good fold equity, and keeps postflop options open.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. While A5o is not a premium hand, it is generally strong enough to defend against a button open, especially with the pot odds offered. Calling allows me to see a flop without over-committing chips with a marginal hand out of position, though I must be cautious of Claude in the Big Blind potentially squeezing.

Folding 2-6o (worst hand in poker) against two opponents. With 2-6o, there's no reasonable justification to call even with adequate pot odds. The hand is dominated by virtually any other holding, and being out of position against two opponents makes post-flop play very difficult. Preserving chips for better opportunities is the correct approach.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a monotone board of hearts. However, my kicker is weak (5), and I do not hold a heart, meaning any fourth heart on the board will make my hand extremely vulnerable. I am also out of position against Minni. Checking is the most prudent move here to control the size of the pot and see how Minni acts on this texture. If she bets, I will likely call one street to re-evaluate on the turn, but I don't want to build a large pot myself with a hand that is likely either way ahead or way behind.

I opened from the button with T♠4♦ and got one caller (SB). Flop 3h 7h Ah completely misses my hand: no pair, no hearts, and virtually no backdoor equity. This board heavily favors the caller’s defending range (lots of Ax, pocket pairs and heart draws), so my continuation-bet would have low fold equity and often put me in multi-street trouble when called or raised. With ~40.8BB and only 700 in the pot I prefer pot control and preserving chips on this unpromising texture — check and see how the SB responds. Plan: fold to meaningful pressure on the flop (only consider calling very tiny probes), and if checked to, evaluate the turn for a delayed bluff or a safe pot-control line.

I have top pair with Aces, but my kicker is weak and the board is monotone with three hearts. Minni checked the flop after raising pre-flop, which often indicates she lacks a strong Ace or a made flush, as those hands usually bet for value or protection on such a texture. By checking the turn, I am maintaining a pot-control strategy. If Minni has a draw or a mid-pair, I want to keep the pot small or induce a bluff. If I bet, I risk folding out her air while only getting called by hands that either beat me or have significant equity (like a high heart).

Turn pairs me with T♠4♦ (board 3h 7h Ah Tc). I improved to top pair but I have a very weak kicker and the Ace on board dominates a large chunk of the SB’s calling range (Ax, sets, two-heart combos that would have flopped a flush, etc.). Flash checked the flop and checked the turn, so his range is wide — includes hands I beat (Kx, Qx, weaker tens, some pocket pairs) but also many hands that beat me. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal holding, yet I should try to extract thin value and deny free rivers to some overcards. I’ll make a small value bet ~1/3 pot (250) to get called by worse hands and keep the pot manageable. If I face a substantial raise I’ll give it up (he’s more likely to have an Ace, a set, or a made flush); if he just calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and proceed cautiously.

On the turn, Minni bets 250 into a 700 pot after checking back on an Ace-high monotone heart flop. My top pair with a weak kicker is a strong candidate for a bluff-catcher here. Her check on the flop makes it less likely she has a flopped flush or a strong Ace, as those hands typically bet for value or protection on such a wet texture. She might be betting now with a Ten, a single heart draw, or a pure bluff. Raising would only isolate me against better hands, so calling is the most effective way to control the pot and see a river while keeping her range wide.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Aces with a 5). The board paired on the river (3s), and there are three hearts from the flop. Minni's check on the flop suggests she is unlikely to have a flopped flush or a premium Ace. However, she bet the turn, indicating some strength or a draw. My hand is a classic bluff-catcher in this spot. I will check to see if she wants to check behind with a marginal hand or attempt a bluff with a missed draw. Betting myself doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and will definitely be called by better ones.

I hold T♠4♦ — top pair but a very weak kicker on a wet texture (3♥7♥A♥ on the flop). I intentionally bet small (250) on the turn to extract thin value and keep the pot controlled; Flash called. The river pairs the board with a 3 (3s), which now gives hands with a single 3 trips and still leaves Ax and two-heart flushes beating me. Flash checked the river, which is consistent with a wide range: some worse hands I beat, but also many hands that still have me crushed. With only marginal showdown value, poor kicker, and limited fold equity versus a deep, calling SB, the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check and see showdown (and fold to any substantial donk shove). I will not bloat the pot further with this marginal holding.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, 7-5o is a very weak starting hand. Minni is currently short-stacked in the big blind (37 BBs) and might be looking for opportunities to shove or defend aggressively, making a steal attempt with such a weak hand unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Small blind vs big blind heads-up situation. 7♠8♠ is a weak hand, but the cost-to-call is minimal (only 50 more chips), and I have a significant stack advantage over Minni's 3,730-chip stack. The hand has decent potential to make straights or pairs on many board textures. Playing more hands from the small blind against a desperate short-stacked opponent is profitable. Folding would be unnecessarily tight given the pot odds and my stack advantage.

Heads-up in the big blind with J♥8♦. Flash folded and Claude merely completed the small blind (wide, marginal range) to make the pot 200; I have no work to do preflop. J8o is playable but not strong enough to thin‑value-raise here — I want to keep the pot under control and see a flop where my jack can have showdown value and I may pick up backdoor draws. Plan: check and evaluate the flop; call small continuation bets with pair/flush/straight prospects and fold to large pressure when I miss.

Betting with a solid flop for 7♠8♠ - I have a made pair of 8s along with multiple draws (flush draw with 4 spades and open-ended straight draw with 5/9). The board of 3♠ 8♥ K♦ is favorable. Minni is dangerously short-stacked (3,730), making a bet a good way to apply pressure and build the pot with a strong hand. Flash is still in with 17,300, but my hand is strong enough to bet for value and fold equity. A 125-chip bet (roughly half the pot) is appropriately sized to accomplish my goals without overcommitting.

I have middle pair (8♦8♥) with a jack kicker on 3♠ 8♥ K♦. Claude was a limp/call preflop (wide, marginal range) and his small flop bet to 125 is a standard probe — it gives me ~28% pot odds to call (125/(325+125)=125/450). Against his range I'm often ahead (worse eights, many Kx/overcards, bluffs and floats), and calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in while controlling the pot. I avoid raising because my kicker and hand strength aren't strong enough to build the pot vs likely Kx or big holdings, and a raise risks committing a sizable portion of my 3,730 stack unnecessarily. Plan: call now, see the turn, and re-evaluate — fold to large turn pressure if the board or action indicates strong Kx/two‑pair; bet for value or protection only if I improve or encounter weakness.

Betting 300 on the turn with my pair of 8s and multiple draws. The turn Ace is a bit concerning but my hand is still solid. Minni's short stack (3,605) makes him vulnerable to continued aggression. This bet builds the pot while maintaining my pressure from the flop. Checking would allow Minni to reach the river without risk and potentially improve his hand. The pot-sized/75% pot bet is appropriately sized to force a critical decision from a short-stacked opponent.

Call 300. I have middle pair (8♦ with J kicker) on 3♠‑8♥‑K♦‑A♣. Claude limp‑called preflop (very wide) and fired a small flop probe (125) which I called to keep his bluffs/worse hands in. His turn raise to 300 is a modest-sized continuation — calling 300 into a 750 pot gives me ~28.6% pot odds (300/(750+300) ≈ 28.6%). Against his limp/call range (Kx, some Ax, small pairs, floats and bluffs) I usually have enough equity to continue, and calling keeps the pot controlled. Raising to the 600 minimum would bloat the pot with a hand that is vulnerable to Ks/As and sets and would unnecessarily commit a meaningful slice of my 3,605 stack. Folding is too weak given the price to see the river. Plan after the call: re-evaluate on the river — fold to large river aggression when I miss (especially if the river pairs the board or he sizes big), but if he checks or gives a small river bet I can extract value from worse eights or call light bluffs; bet for value only if I improve or find clear weakness.

Checking on the river with a pair of 8s on a K-A-T board. Minni is critically short-stacked (3,305) and checked to me. My pair is decent but vulnerable to K, A, or T holdings. Minni's line suggests either a marginal hand or he's given up. Checking secures the pot and gets to showdown without additional risk. The scary board texture makes value betting less attractive—I'm better off securing what I've won rather than risking more chips against a potentially better hand.

I have middle pair (8♣/8♦ — I hold 8♦ with 8♥ on board) with a jack kicker on 3♠–8♥–K♦–A♣–T♥. Claude limp‑called preflop (very wide), c‑bet a small 125 on the flop and at the turn bet 300 which I called — that line suggests a lot of medium‑strength hands (Kx, Ax, small pairs) and some floats/bluffs. He now checks the river.

A couple of points: my pair of eights is vulnerable to most of Claude’s value range (any Kx, Ax, sets, and the QJ straight combo), and there are very few worse hands that will call a river probe — most worse pairs or marginal holdings would have folded earlier to his betting. Betting here would be a thin value bet that will be called by many hands that beat me and will only fold the pure air I want to fold out; raising is simply out of the question. With my stack (~3,305) and Claude’s very deep stack, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal, easily dominated hand.

Given the board texture, his line, and pot control considerations, the lowest‑variance, most +EV play is to check and take my showdown. If he had a bluff he could still bet, in which case I’ll reassess sizing, but as of now I’ll check and show down.

6-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. While I have position on the button, this hand has no playable value. Even with a cheap call of 100 chips, 6-2o has poor equity against most ranges and is too weak to profitably play. Folding is standard here. With 18,345 chips, I can afford to wait for premium hands and not dilute my stack with marginal situations.

I'm in the small blind (50 posted) with 2♠3♣ — a very weak, unsuited hand and out of position. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to call (total commit 100). Versus a single big blind I'm getting good pot odds to see a flop and Flash defends fairly wide in this game, so calling to realize equity is the standard play. I won't raise with this hand OOP. Postflop plan: check/fold to significant aggression unless I pair or pick up a strong draw (open-ender or two pair), otherwise see a cheap flop and conserve chips for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has just completed the bet, and the Button folded. I have position for the rest of the hand. While Ten-Nine is a decent hand to raise with for value and to take control of the pot, checking allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small against a shorter stack. I will check and evaluate the flop.

I have 2♠3♣ and completely missed the Qc‑Kh‑7s flop — no pair and only remote backdoor outs. I’m out of position against a fairly wide‑defending big blind who already checked; there’s no reason to invest chips with essentially no equity. I’ll check to control the pot and plan to fold to any meaningful bet. I only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw (or get a very cheap turn and a clear read that Flash will fold to aggression).

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit. The board is King-Queen-Seven, which doesn't hit my hand. I have a very weak backdoor straight draw. Minni, the Small Blind, has checked. Since I have no showdown value and the board texture likely hits a lot of a completing range (like Kx or Qx), I will check back to see a free turn card. Betting here doesn't accomplish much against a short stack who might check-call with any piece of the board.

I have 2♠3♣ on Qc‑Kh‑7s‑Kc — absolutely nothing. I’m out of position, the pot is small (200) and the turn pairs the board (heavy Kx/Qx range) while offering no realistic draw for my hand. Betting here is low‑EV: opponents will often call or raise with any king/queen or decent showdown hand, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with near‑zero equity. I’ll check and preserve my 4.3k stack; fold to any bet and only continue if I pair on the river or get a very clear read.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Nine offsuit on a board of King-Queen-Seven-King. There was no pre-flop action as Minni just completed from the Small Blind and I checked. On the flop, both of us checked. Now on the turn, the King has paired. Minni has checked for a second time on the board. While I have no showdown value with Ten-high, Minni's short stack makes her more likely to be sticky with any piece of the board (like a Seven or a Queen) or even Ace-high. Since the pot is very small (200 chips) and I have a gutshot draw to a straight (if a Jack hits), I will check behind to see a free river. I'm not looking to bluff into a potential trap or a stubborn short stack in this specific spot.

I hold 2♠3♣ and never improved on Qc–Kh–7s–Kc–6s. No pair, no real draw, and I’m out of position. Flash checked both flop and turn, so his range is likely wide but contains a lot of hands that will call a river bluff (Kx/Qx, pairs, some showdown hands). The pot is small (200) relative to our stacks, so a bluff here has poor fold equity and is low EV. I’ll check and conserve chips — fold to any meaningful river bet and wait for a better spot.

The board is Kc-Kh-Qc-7s-6s and the action has been checked all the way through. I have Ten-high, which is unlikely to win at showdown. However, the pot is very small (200 chips) and Minni is playing a relatively short stack. While a bluff might force out some better hands like Jack-high or small pairs, the reward doesn't justify the risk of being called by a stubborn short stack with a marginal hand like a 7 or Ace-high. I will check and take my chance at a free showdown.

I’m on the button with 9♠8♦ and 42bb effective in a 3‑handed pot. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to break even vs two opponents, which 9‑8o doesn’t reliably have, while a min‑raise to 200 is too small and invites lots of action/3‑bets. I want to seize the initiative and use position, so I’ll open to a standard 3x (300) to get fold equity and play post‑flop in position if called. If either blind 3‑bets large I’ll fold and preserve my stack; if called I’ll C‑bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on high‑card boards.

Ace-Trey offsuit is a marginal but playable hand in the Small Blind against a Button open in a 3-handed game. While 3-betting is a strong option to exert pressure and potentially take down the pot immediately, I choose to call to keep the pot size manageable and see a flop. This approach avoids overcommitting against Minni's 40BB stack, which might shove over a 3-bet, forcing me to fold away my equity. I'll evaluate the board texture and Minni's post-flop aggression to determine my next move.

Fold 8-6 offsuit from the big blind in a three-way pot. This is a weak hand with insufficient equity against both Minni's raising range and Flash's calling range. Even though Minni's short stack suggests wide raises, Flash's presence in the pot makes this unprofitable. With a solid chip lead and 73 hands remaining, there's no value in playing weak hands out of position in multiway pots.

On this Ace-high board, I have hit Top Pair with a weak kicker (3). The board is quite coordinated with both flush and straight draws possible. Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser to see how they react. Leading out here might force out worse hands while getting called by better Aces or draws that have significant equity. By checking, I can keep the pot manageable and better evaluate Minni's range based on their bet sizing or decision to check back.

I raised preflop and was called; flop A♣ 9♣ T♠ gives me middle pair (9♠). Flash (SB) checked — he can have a wide range (missed overcards, pocket pairs, club draws or the A/T). With position I want to apply pressure to fold out pure air and charge club/straight draws while getting value from worse pairs and broadways. I’ll make a half‑pot c-bet to accomplish that without bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold; if I’m called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (Trey) is very weak. The board is wet with straight and flush possibilities. Minni, opening from the Button, has a wide range that includes many hands I currently beat, such as smaller pairs, air, or draws. Her half-pot continuation bet is standard. Folding top pair here would be too tight, but raising is also unattractive as it likely only gets called by better Aces (AJ, AQ, AK) or made hands like two-pair or sets. Calling is the best option to control the pot size, keep her bluffs in, and re-evaluate on the turn.

On this turn, the five of hearts is a total blank and doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still have top pair with an extremely weak kicker. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who also bet the flop, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the pot size and observe Minni's reaction. If she checks back, it reinforces that my hand might be good; if she bets, I can re-evaluate based on her sizing, but I generally want to avoid building a massive pot with Ace-Trey on this coordinated board.

I check to control the pot and realize equity. On A♣ 9♣ T♠ 5♥ I have middle pair (9♠). Flash’s check-call on the flop indicates a wide range (Ax, Tx, club draws, some pairs). A significant portion of that range already beats me (A, T, sets), and there are few worse hands that will pay off a turn bet. Betting here mainly charges draws and gets called by better hands, risking a larger pot when I’m often behind. With ~3.6k effective and a 1.4k pot I prefer to keep the pot manageable, see the river in position, and re-evaluate. If he checks back I can look for a small value bet on many rivers; if he fires a small river bet I can call as a bluff-catcher, but I’ll fold to a large polarized bet that strongly represents A/flush.

On the river, the board has completed several potential draws including a flush (three clubs) and multiple straights (76 and QJ). I have top pair with a very weak kicker (3). Minni's check on the turn suggests she might have a medium-strength hand or a draw she was happy to take a free card with. Given the board texture, my hand has significantly decreased in relative value. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I risk being raised by a flush or a better Ace. By checking, I can potentially see a cheap showdown or decide whether to call a bet based on her sizing, though I'm mostly looking to check-fold if she shows significant strength on this card.

River: I’ve made two pair (9s and 8d) on A♣ 9♣ T♠ 5♥ 8♣. Villain checked the flop-call line, I checked the turn and he checked the river — a fairly passive line that contains a wide range (many single-pair hands, some Tx/Ax, some club combos and a few sets). My two-pair is good vs a lot of that range but loses to Ax/TX two-pair, sets and any completed club flush if he holds two clubs. I want to extract thin value from worse one-pair hands and charge any stubborn marginal calls while keeping the pot controlled against hands that beat me, so I make a modest value bet of ~half the pot. If he raises big I’ll fold (my hand is vulnerable to polarized raises); if he calls I’ll see showdown.

On the river, the board has become quite coordinated with the completion of the club flush and potential straights (QJ or 76). However, I have top pair with an Ace. The action follows a pattern: Minni bet the flop, checked the turn, and is now betting 700 on the river into a pot of approximately 1400. This 'bet-check-bet' line often indicates a medium-strength hand like a marginal Ace or a Ten, or occasionally a missed draw being turned into a bluff. While many hands in Minni's range beat my Ace-Trey (especially better kickers, two pairs, or the newly completed flushes), the pot odds are roughly 3:1. In a three-handed game, folding top pair for a single bet on the river is generally too tight unless I have a very strong read. I will call to see the showdown, as my hand still has enough equity against her wide button-opening range and potential thin value bets.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit. In three-handed play, this is a standard hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy, and a raise to 300 has been my consistent sizing for opening pots.

Folding Q6o from the small blind facing a button raise. This hand lacks the strength and playability needed to justify playing out of position against an aggressive opponent. While I have marginal equity, the positional disadvantage post-flop makes this unprofitable. With a healthy stack, I can afford to wait for stronger hands in better spots.

Facing a button open to 300 from Flash with the SB folded. I have Qc4h in the big blind — a marginal offsuit queen. Folding is too nitty given the pot odds and Flash’s frequent 3bb raises, but 3-betting is unnecessary and exploitable with this hand. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) gives ~31% break-even equity (200 / 650) and lets me see a flop with queen-high showdown potential and some backdoor possibilities. Plan: don’t inflate the pot preflop, evaluate flop (proceed if I pair the Q or pick up strong draws; otherwise pot-control and fold to significant aggression).

Flop: Kd Qs 4c — I hold Qc4h: two pair (Queens and Fours). This is a very strong hand versus the button’s wide opening range; only sets (KK, QQ) or KQ two‑pair beat me, and those combos are relatively rare. Flash is a frequent opener, so his range contains a lot of Kx, Qx, broadways and medium pairs that will pay off a value bet. I want to extract and deny equity to turn cards/draws, so betting is the right plan rather than checking behind and giving a free card. I’ll size for value but not overcommit: bet ~2/3 pot (650 pot → bet 400). This price will get calls from Kx, worse Qx and many pocket pairs, and still leaves room to call a raise if he has one of the few hands that beat me. Action: bet 400.

I have top pair with a King and a 7 kicker on a board of 4c-Kd-Qs. Pre-flop, I raised from the button and Minni called from the big blind. Minni has now led out (donk bet) for 400 into a pot of 650. While my kicker is weak, top pair in a three-handed game is too strong to fold to a single bet. Raising might isolate me against better Kings (like KJ, KT, or KQ) or sets, whereas calling allows Minni to continue with bluffs or weaker hands. Given Minni's relatively short stack and her tendency to be active in pots, I will call to evaluate the turn and keep the pot size manageable.

I have Qc4h giving me two pair (Queens and Fours) after the flop bet was called. Flash is a frequent, wide opener and his call on the flop indicates a lot of Kx, Qx, broadways and medium pairs — very often dominated by my two pair. The turn 7s is a largely harmless card (doesn’t complete obvious straights or flushes for the typical calling range), so this is a good spot to extract value rather than check and give a free river. The pot is 1,450; a bet of ~2/3–70% pot prices in worse Kx, worse Qx and pocket pairs while not overcommitting my stack. I’ll bet 1,000 to maximize value and deny equity to runner-runner combos. If Flash raises substantially (polarized to sets/KQ), I’ll re-evaluate — willing to call a small/medium raise but fold to a shove that clearly represents the few combos that beat me.

On the turn, the 7 of spades has improved my hand to two pair (Kings and Sevens). Minni has led out on both the flop and the turn, which often indicates a strong made hand or a draw in a three-handed game. Given that I now beat all of her single-pair King hands (like KJ or KT) and most of her semi-bluffs, I want to raise for value and to protect against the draws now present on the board (spades and the J-10 straight draw). A raise to 3000 significantly increases the pot and commits Minni's relatively short stack, maximizing my potential to double up or take down a large pot before a potentially dangerous river card falls.

I have two pair (Qs and 4s) on a K♦ Q♠ 4♣ 7♠ board — a very strong, relatively “locked-up” hand. Only a narrow set of hands beat me (KK, QQ, 44) and KQ two‑pair; those are limited combos (~11 combos total). The turn raise is big, but Flash is a frequent, wide opener and his flop call/turn raise will include many Kx and other worse hands and some bluffs. Pot odds to call are favorable: I must call 2,000 to win a pot of 5,450 (break‑even equity ≈ 26.9%). My two‑pair equity versus a realistic raising range is well above that, so folding would be throwing away significant +EV. Shoving/raising isn’t necessary (the min‑raise is 5,000 which is > my stack — an all‑in would be too high-variance and would fold out some of the hands I want to keep), so the best line is to call, keep worse hands and bluffs in the pot, and re-evaluate on the river. Action: call the 2,000 (total committed on the hand after calling = 3,700).

I have Qc4h for two pair (Qs + 4s) on K♦ Q♠ 4♣ 7♠ 6♠. This is a very strong hand vs a wide, button-style opening range — only a narrow set of hands beat me (KK, QQ, 44) or Kx hands that make KQ or K4 two‑pair. Flash’s line (open, call on the flop, a big turn raise, then check the river) is polarized — it contains both strong value hands and missed draws/bluffs. Betting the river can extract value from worse hands, but with my short remaining stack (2,005) a large bet or shove is high-variance and will fold out some of the hands I want to keep. Conversely a small bet risks either folding out thin callers or getting check-raised by the few hands that beat me.

Given the turn raise and his passive river check, pot-control is the prudent exploitative play: check to force showdowns or induce him to value-bet himself. If he bets the river I can call with comfortable pot odds and reevaluate; if he checks behind I take down a big pot without risking my stack to a river raise. Therefore I will check.

On the river, the board is 4c-Kd-Qs-7s-6s. I have two pair (Kings and Sevens). Minni has checked to me after calling my significant raise on the turn. While the 6s on the river is a potential scare card (completing a backdoor flush or an unlikely straight with 5-8), the pot is already very large (7450) relative to Minni's remaining stack (2005). Given that I have a very strong hand in three-handed play and Minni is getting roughly 5:1 odds to call, she is likely to call with any Top Pair hand (like KJ or KT) or even a strong Queen. I will bet her remaining stack for value, as checking back would miss an opportunity to extract the last of her chips when I am likely ahead.

I have Qc4h — two pair (Qs and 4s) on K♦ Q♠ 4♣ 7♠ 6♠. Flash bets 2,005 and I must call 2,005 (this is my full remaining stack). Calling would commit a total of 3,700 (already committed) + 2,005 = 5,705 chips. The call only requires ~17.5% equity (2,005 / (9,455 + 2,005) ≈ 17.5%) to be break-even. Hands that actually beat me are narrow: KK, KQ, K4, 44 and completed spade flushes — relative to a wide button raising/calling range those are a minority. Many hands in his line (single-pair Kx that don’t make two pair, worse Qx, medium pairs or busted turn draws) are behind me or will call a small river bet. His small river sizing is consistent with trying to get called by worse hands rather than polarize to only the nuts. Raising/shoving myself is unnecessary — it would fold out thin value and only inflate variance. Folding throws away a very strong, high-EV hand. Therefore I call the river all-in to realize my equity and extract value from worse hands and bluffs.

In heads-up play on the button with Jack-Six offsuit, I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (150 pot, 50 to call). This is a marginal hand but worth seeing a flop in heads-up dynamics where position and postflop play matter significantly. Jack-Six is within a reasonable calling range for button play, and I have decent stack depth (18,145 chips) to play the hand postflop. Folding here would be too tight. I'll call and evaluate the flop based on board texture and opponent's likely holdings. Flash has been fairly aggressive throughout the session, so I can exploit that with good positional awareness postflop.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (6 of Spades, 3 of Hearts). Claude has limped from the Small Blind. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check to see if I can connect with the board for free, rather than trying to bluff or folding a hand that has already contributed to the pot.

I am first to act on a monotone flop of King, Seven, and Deuce of Spades. I hold the Six of Spades, giving me a weak flush draw. Given that this is a small, limped pot and my hand has very little showdown value, checking is the most sensible play to see a free turn card and evaluate Claude's response.

Flash checked the flop, showing weakness in heads-up play. With position and jack-high, I have the initiative. A bet of 120 (roughly 60% pot) is a reasonable semi-bluff that puts pressure on Flash's weak holding while keeping my own risk manageable relative to my 18,095 chip stack. The jack gives me decent equity if called, and I can continue exploiting Flash's check with positional advantage.

I am facing a bet of 120 into a pot of 200 on a monotone flop of King, Seven, and Deuce of Spades. I hold the Six of Spades, giving me a flush draw. Although it is a low flush draw, the pot odds (requiring approximately 27% equity) and the potential to improve to a flush or see how the turn develops make a call profitable in this small, limped pot. I will call to see the turn card.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds pairs the board. I am out of position and still holding a low flush draw (Six of Spades). In a limped pot, I want to keep the pot small given that the board has paired, which makes flushes more vulnerable to full houses, and my specific flush draw is very weak. Checking is the most prudent play to see how Claude reacts and potentially realize my equity cheaply.

Checking down with jack-high on a paired, spade-heavy board. Flash has shown weakness with two checks but called my flop bet, indicating some hand strength. With only J6o, I lack the equity to profitably bet against a caller. Checking allows me to reach the river cheaply, avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand, and keep Flash's range wide. If Flash bets the river, I can reassess based on the action pattern and final board card. This is solid pot control with a weak holding.

On the river, the board completes a four-flush with the Trey of Spades. I hold the Six of Spades, which gives me a very low flush. The board is [Ks, 7s, 2s, Kd, 3s]. Any opponent holding a spade higher than the six (Ace, Queen, Jack, Ten, Nine, or Eight) would have me beaten. Given that Claude limped pre-flop and bet the flop, he could easily be holding a middle spade. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands (only the Four or Five of Spades), and I risk being raised by a superior flush or a full house (if he has a King). Checking allows me to see if Claude checks behind with a hand like a King or a weaker pair, or to evaluate his bet size if he decides to bluff or value bet a better flush.

River check with J-high on a paired, four-spade board. Flash showed weakness with a river check but demonstrated hand strength by calling my flop bet. J6o is too weak to profitably bluff - if Flash calls, I'm likely beaten by any pair or reasonable high card. Checking allows me to reach showdown at minimal cost, and there's some chance Flash has completely bricked or has air worse than my jack-high. The risk/reward of a river bluff doesn't justify risking additional chips with this marginal holding.

In a heads-up situation from the button, I have a wide opening range. Jack-Trey suited (J3s) is a hand that performs well enough to merit an open raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Calling with J9s in the BB against a button raise. Strong pot odds (2:1), decent hand with position postflop, and Flash's aggressive tendencies suggest a wide opening range. This is a standard play to see the flop in a favorable situation.

Betting top pair (Jh) with a 9 kicker on a KdQd board after Flash checked. This is a standard value bet to extract value from weaker holdings and gain information. The half-pot-sized bet is appropriately sized given our effective stacks and the need to build a pot with a moderately strong hand on a dangerous board. Flash's check from button suggests weakness or a medium-strength hand, making this a good opportunity to take the lead.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with a Jack-high flush draw on a very coordinated board (K-Q-J). Claude has led out for a half-pot bet of 300. While the board is heavy with Broadway cards that likely hit a Big Blind's calling range, my hand has significant equity between the pair and the flush draw. Calling is the most prudent action here to see the turn and keep the pot manageable, as raising might bloat the pot against a range that could already contain straights or two-pair hands.

Betting 600 on the turn with top pair (J9) after Flash checked. The semi-coordinated board (K♦Q♦) and Flash's check suggest I have the initiative. This bet: (1) extracts value from weaker pairs and draws, (2) protects against free cards to completion, (3) maintains my flop betting narrative, and (4) is appropriately sized at ~50% pot to build the pot while preserving stack flexibility for river decisions. The 6♠ is a blank unlikely to improve Flash's calling range significantly.

On a board of Js-Kd-Qd-6s, I have a pair of Jacks and a diamond flush draw. Claude led on the flop and is now firing a second barrel of 600 into a 1200 pot. While my third pair is weak given the Broadway-heavy board, my flush draw provides significant equity. I need 25% equity to call (600 to win a total pot of 2400), and my 9 diamond outs give me roughly 19.5% equity just to the flush, not accounting for potential outs with a Jack or the implied odds if I hit my draw. Given the deep stacks, calling is the most profitable play to see if I can improve on the river.

Betting 1200 (50% pot) with King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K) on the river. Flash checked, showing weakness or a medium-strength hand. This bet size extracts value while remaining reasonable enough to get called by weaker holdings like medium pairs or marginal hands. A King-high straight is a monster hand that beats all pairs, two pairs, and three of a kind. Only an Ace-high straight or flush beats me, both unlikely given Flash's line of play (aggressive pre-flop but checked on river). Standard value betting with a premium hand on the river after opponent shows weakness.

The river card is the Ten of Clubs (Tc), completing a four-card straight on the board (K-Q-J-T). Any Ace or Nine now makes a straight. My hand, a pair of Jacks with a missed diamond flush draw, is extremely weak in this context. Claude has led out on all three streets (donk-betting the flop and continuing on the turn and river). This triple-barrel line on such a coordinated board typically represents a very strong range, likely at least a King, Two Pair, or a completed straight. Given the board texture and the betting action, my third pair is purely a bluff-catcher, and a poor one at that, since many of Claude's semi-bluffs (like 9x or Ax) would have arrived on the river. I am folding to preserve my stack.

In heads-up play from the button (small blind), I'm getting favorable pot odds of 3:1 to call just 50 more chips. While 2c-Jh is a weak hand, several factors support calling: (1) Button position gives me significant post-flop advantage and last-action benefit; (2) The minimal cost (50 chips) is negligible against my 19125 stack; (3) In heads-up play, ranges are much wider and I can exploit position to control the hand post-flop; (4) I have a Jack, which could easily be best on many flops in heads-up. Folding weak hands too often in heads-up from the button is exploitable. I'll call and play positionally post-flop.

I am in the big blind with Queen-seven offsuit. The opponent in the small blind has limped into the pot. While Q7o is a decent hand in heads-up play, there is little incentive to bloat the pot out of position against a limp. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and evaluate the strength of my hand based on the board texture.

I have Queen high with a backdoor club draw on a flop of 5c Jc Ts. In a limped pot where the opponent checked from the small blind, there is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff yet. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and potentially improve or re-evaluate based on the next card.

Checking is the correct play with J-2 on this coordinated 5c-Jc-Ts board. My pair of Jacks is vulnerable to multiple draws and made hands in Flash's range. Flash's check suggests weakness, but betting J-2 here would be overcommitting with a weak kicker. By checking, I maintain my positional advantage for the turn while avoiding unnecessary chips with a hand that is neither strong enough to build a pot with nor worth folding. I'll have better information and decision-making opportunities on the turn and river.

On the turn, the 8d gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 9 for a J-high straight). The pot is very small (200) and my opponent has checked twice. While I could bet as a semi-bluff, checking back allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity or evaluate a bluff on the river if I miss. There is no need to bloat a tiny pot with Queen high out of position.

Flash has checked both the flop and turn, indicating a weak hand in his range. With top pair (J-2), I'm likely ahead. In heads-up play from position, betting the turn builds the pot with the likely best hand, gets value from Flash's draws or worse holdings, and avoids giving free cards. A bet of 150 (roughly 3/4 pot) is appropriately sized to apply pressure while remaining sustainable. If Flash calls, I'll reassess on the river based on his action pattern.

On the turn, the 8d provides me with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). However, Claude has bet 150 into a 200-chip pot, which is a significant 75% pot-sized bet. With only Queen-high and 4 outs to a straight (approximately 8.7% equity), I do not have the necessary pot odds to call (I would need about 30% equity to make a breakeven call). Since I am out of position and have very little showdown value, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with Js4s, which is a suited hand with some connectivity and a high card (Jack). In a heads-up or three-handed context, this is a strong opening hand from the small blind. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the big blind (Claude).

Calling with 4-10 offsuit in heads-up BB against Flash's standard raise. The 2:1 pot odds combined with post-flop position advantage makes this a reasonable call with a marginal hand. Flash has been aggressive throughout, and his button raises should be wide in heads-up play. I can make decisions based on the flop texture and his action.

Checking with marginal hand (4-10 offsuit) on a King-Queen board where I have no equity. The Kings and Queen heavily favor Flash's button-raiser's range. Checking allows me to evaluate Flash's c-bet action before committing more chips. If he bets, I'll fold, as I have no realistic draws and minimal equity. This is a chip-preservation play in heads-up on an unfavorable flop texture.

On a board of Qs-Kc-Kh, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture is very difficult for the big blind to connect with unless they have a King, a Queen, or a straight draw like JT. By placing a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot), I put pressure on Claude's weaker holdings like low-to-mid pocket pairs or total air. If called, I have some backdoor equity with the Jack of Spades, but my primary goal is to take the pot down immediately.

Folding 4-10 offsuit on a KK-Q board. This hand has virtually no equity against Flash's betting range - no made hand, no real draws (only a gutshot), and the paired board heavily favors a button-raiser's hand composition. Even with 4:1 pot odds, the equity isn't there to justify the call. This is a standard chip-preservation fold on an unfavorable texture.

In heads-up play from the button (small blind), I should be raising with a wide range of hands. 97s (9-7 suited) is a decent holding with two cards of the same suit, giving me flexibility and backdoor draw potential. I have positional advantage as the button/small blind, which is crucial in heads-up poker. A raise to 250 represents roughly a 2.5x opening from the big blind, which is standard for button play. This puts pressure on my opponent while still keeping the pot manageable if called. The stacks are relatively even, so aggressive position play is appropriate here.

In a heads-up situation, Queen-Seven offsuit is a standard defending hand from the Big Blind against a 2.5x button raise. I am getting 3.3-to-1 odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 500), which requires roughly 30% equity. Q7o typically has over 45% equity against a wide button opening range. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the price is too good to fold.

The flop of As-Jc-9c is quite unfavorable for my specific hand (Qd 7h). I have no pair and no real draws on a board that coordinates well and contains an Ace, which is a major part of the pre-flop raiser's range. Leading into the aggressor here makes little sense given my lack of equity. I will check and evaluate based on my opponent's action, though I am likely folding to any bet.

I'm c-betting with top pair and a flush draw on a flop where Flash checked and showed weakness. A bet of 325 (roughly 65% of pot) is standard aggression in heads-up play - strong enough to apply pressure and define Flash's hand, but not so large that I blow away the hand if he's weak. If Flash has a marginal hand, he'll fold. If he has something better, I still have significant draw equity with my pair and 4-flush. This is a profitable bet given the strength of my holding and Flash's passive action.

The flop of As-Jc-9c is extremely disconnected from my Q7o. My opponent, the pre-flop aggressor, has a range that contains many Aces, Jacks, and Nines, as well as flush and straight draws. Having no pair and no real draw to improve, calling a bet that is over half the pot is not justifiable. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up scenario, Ten-Deuce offsuit is one of the weakest hands in the range. Although the Button/Small Blind generally plays a wide range of hands, T2o has very poor equity and playability post-flop. Given that the stacks are deep (around 200BB), there is no need to commit chips with such a marginal hand. Folding is a disciplined choice to wait for better opportunities.

Button play in heads-up with near-equal stacks requires positional aggression. Queen-four offsuit is within my opening range from the button and benefits from last-action advantage. A 2x raise to 200 applies pressure while maintaining standard sizing. This allows me to build the pot when ahead, deny Flash implied odds, and control the postflop action with positional advantage.

In Hand #85, I am in the Big Blind with 7s 4s. Claude, on the Button/Small Blind, has raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot is 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Suited hands, even low ones like 74s, have decent playability and equity in heads-up situations against a wide button-opening range. Given the deep stacks and the price offered, folding is too conservative, and 3-betting is unnecessary. I will call to defend my big blind and evaluate on the flop.

I have hit top pair on a 7-6-3 rainbow-ish board (two clubs). In a heads-up pot, top pair is a solid hand, even with a weak kicker like a 4. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I plan to call a standard continuation bet from Claude to see a turn card, as my hand is currently ahead of most of his overcard range.

With Q♠4♥ on a 7♣6♣3♥ flop, I hold just queen-high with no draws or pair. While positional advantage typically calls for aggression when the out-of-position player checks, my hand strength here is extremely marginal. Flash's check could represent weakness, but it could also be a trap with a made hand (paired board cards) or a draw. Given that Q4o is at the bottom of my opening range and I have limited equity against Flash's big blind defense range, committing chips into the pot with this hand would be suboptimal. Checking allows me to see another card without investing additional chips on a marginal holding, preserving my stack for better opportunities while remaining in position for the turn.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds appears, which is an overcard to my pair of sevens. After Claude checked back on the flop (7-6-3), his range is likely composed of overcards (like AK, AJ, KJ) or small-to-medium pocket pairs. A Queen is a card that he could potentially have, but his flop check makes it less likely he was protecting a strong hand. I will check again to control the pot size and see if he decides to bet now that an overcard has arrived. Checking allows me to evaluate his action; if he checks behind, I am likely still ahead.

On the turn with Q♠4♥ and the board now reading 7♣6♣3♥Q♦, I've improved to a pair of queens—a significant upgrade from my previous queen-high holding. 

Flash has shown weakness through consecutive checks on both the flop and turn. This pattern suggests either a weak unpaired hand, a drawing holding, or air that missed the flop entirely. The queen on the turn is favorable for my opening range and likely gives me the best hand in this spot.

In heads-up play from the button against an opponent showing weakness, positional aggression is warranted. A bet of 250 (roughly 60% of the pot) maintains good pot control while applying meaningful pressure:
- It's strong enough to fold out marginal holdings and air
- It extracts value from hands like unpaired paint cards or small pairs
- It keeps the pot manageable if called, avoiding commitment with a hand that's strong but not premium
- It wins the pot outright often enough given Flash's demonstrated weakness

This sizing balances value extraction with risk management on a vulnerable middle-pair holding.

On the turn, the Queen of Diamonds appeared and Claude bet 250 after checking the flop. My pair of sevens is now second pair. However, Claude's check on the flop (7-6-3) makes it less likely he holds a strong hand like an overpair or a strong seven. His bet on the turn looks like a delayed continuation bet, potentially representing the Queen or simply trying to take the pot after I checked twice. Given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 900) and the likelihood that my pair of sevens is still ahead of many of his air/overcard hands, I will call to see the river and evaluate his final action.

On the river Ten of Spades, the board reads 7c-6c-3h-Qd-Ts. I have a pair of sevens with a weak kicker. Claude checked back the flop but bet the turn when the Queen arrived, which I called. The river Ten is another overcard to my pair. My hand has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value. By checking, I can see a showdown for free if Claude checks back, or I can evaluate a bet if he decides to bluff with missed draws like 5-4 or 9-8. Betting would likely only result in being called by better hands like a Queen, a Ten, or a higher pocket pair.

River decision with Q♠4♥ on final board 7♣6♣3♥Q♦T♠. I've made a pair of queens with a weak kicker. Flash has demonstrated consistent weakness across all streets—checking the flop, turn, and now the river. This pattern strongly suggests Flash either has marginal unpaired holdings (K-high, J-high), a missed draw, or complete air. The ten on the river is a safe card that doesn't substantially change the dynamics.

Given my hand strength (pair of queens is likely best here), positional advantage, and opponent weakness, this is a clear value-betting spot. A bet of 400 (roughly 45% of the pot) extracts maximum value while remaining reasonable:

- It wins the pot outright often enough given Flash's demonstrated weakness
- It extracts value from hands I beat (unpaired overcards, small pairs, whiffed draws)
- It's appropriately sized—large enough to count as meaningful value extraction, not so large it becomes reckless
- Heads-up play demands aggressive value extraction when weakness is shown
- I maintain healthy stack control and positioning for future hands

This is optimal risk-adjusted value extraction.

On the river Ten of Spades, the board is 7c-6c-3h-Qd-Ts. I have a pair of sevens with a weak kicker (4). Claude checked the flop, bet the turn (Qd), and is now betting 400 into a 900 pot on the river. My pair of sevens is now third pair. Claude's line (Check-back flop, bet Turn, bet River) often represents either a thin value hand that improved (like a Queen or a Ten) or a bluff from missed straight draws (like 5-4, 8-9, or 5-8). Given that Claude checked the flop, he is less likely to have a premium overpair. The pot odds are 3.25:1 (calling 400 to win a total pot of 1300), meaning I only need to be right about 24% of the time. In a heads-up match, folding a pair of sevens to a half-pot bet after showing weakness can be exploited. I will call to catch potential bluffs from missed draws.

In a heads-up situation on the button, King-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to open for a raise. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with a range advantage.

Flash raised to 300 from the button/small blind in heads-up play. I hold Q9o in the big blind, which is a marginal but playable hand in this context. With only 200 more to call and a pot that will be 600, I'm getting 3:1 odds. Q9 has decent backdoor potential with straight and flush possibilities, and in aggressive heads-up play against Flash (who has been raising frequently and playing wide ranges), calling is appropriate. This hand plays reasonably well post-flop with position on later streets. Folding too much here would allow Flash to exploit my big blind too easily.

Betting Q9o on the 5s-6s-Ts flop. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to K or 4), two overs, and backdoor flush potential. Flash's check indicates weakness. In heads-up play, betting with this combination of immediate win potential and drawing equity is standard. A 200-chip bet (roughly 1/3 pot) applies pressure while maintaining reasonable pot control. This hand has approximately 30-35% equity and benefits from taking down the pot immediately or building it when I improve.

On a monotone board of all spades, Claude has led out with a small bet. I hold the King of Diamonds and Seven of Clubs, which gives me no spades and essentially no equity on this texture. Given that I have no piece of the board and no flush draw, there is no reason to continue in the hand even for a small price. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In heads-up play on the button, I'm in the small blind and should play aggressively with marginal hands. J6o is above-average for heads-up poker (position + aggression matter more than hand strength in these situations). Looking at Flash's recent patterns, he raises frequently but folds to pressure. Rather than just calling the 50 more, I'll raise to 250 total to apply pressure and either win the blind immediately or proceed with position advantage if called. This is a standard aggressive play in heads-up situations where range advantage and positional play are crucial. The hand has some showdown value with J-high, and the raise gives me multiple ways to win the pot.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Claude opened for 2.5x from the Button. While calling is an option to keep the pot smaller out of position, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and extract value from weaker Aces and various King-high or Queen-high hands in Claude's opening range. I will raise to 850 to put pressure on his range and define the hand strength early.

Folding J6o to Flash's 3-bet. While aggressive play is important in heads-up, J6o is too weak to call a 3-bet out of position. My equity is insufficient against Flash's re-raise range, and the pot odds don't justify the call. This is a disciplined fold of a marginal hand in an unfavorable spot.

In a heads-up situation, position is highly valuable. While Queen-Five offsuit is a mediocre hand, it is strong enough to open for a raise from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Claude has shown a mix of defending and folding to pre-flop aggression. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win it without seeing a flop. If I am re-raised, I can comfortably fold given the hand's limited playability against a strong range.

Folding 8-4o (the worst hand in poker) against a button raise. Despite getting 2:1 odds, this hand is too weak to play out of position against an aggressive opponent. Strong poker requires selective hand selection and disciplined folding with trash holdings.

Heads-up play requires opening a wide range from the button. While 4d7d is weak, it has straight potential and I have superior positional advantage for all post-flop streets. A raise to 250 (2.5x big blind) is a standard button opening raise in heads-up, allowing me to take down the blinds or play a pot with position. Folding would be too passive given the positional advantage.

In a heads-up situation with a 200 BB stack, defending the big blind with T7o against a 2.5x open from the small blind is a standard play. The pot odds are favorable, requiring only 150 more to compete for a 350-chip pot. Claude has been active with opens, and T7o has enough high-card value and connectivity to see a flop and navigate post-flop play.

On a paired board of Jc-6d-6h, I am first to act as the Big Blind. I have no piece of the board with Ten-high and no real draws. Checking is the most standard play here to see how the opponent reacts on this texture, as leading out without a hand or a strong draw is unnecessary in a heads-up pot of this size.

With 4d7d on a Jc-6d-6h flop, I have minimal equity and no made hand. Flash's check suggests weakness, but betting with such a weak holding is not justified. I have very limited outs (gutshots, low pairs). Checking back keeps the pot small, allows me to see the turn card relatively cheaply, and maintains flexibility. In heads-up play, checking with weak hands preserves my image and prevents unnecessary bluff-offs.
