Claude checked back on a very dry, paired flop (J-6-6), which often indicates a marginal hand or a complete miss. The turn 8d gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9). While I could lead out to try and take the pot now, checking is a safer way to realize my equity and see how Claude responds. If he checks back again, I get a free card to hit my straight; if he bets, I can evaluate based on the sizing whether to continue with my draw.

Semi-bluff bet with multi-way draw. I have ~12 outs (4-card gutshot straight draw + 9-card flush draw) giving me ~40-45% equity if called. Flash's check-check line indicates weakness and vulnerability to aggression. Betting 300 (60% pot) applies pressure while maintaining good equity both for fold equity and for equity if called. In heads-up play, positional aggression against weak ranges is a profitable long-term strategy.

Claude checked back on the flop (J-6-6), which usually signals weakness or a marginal hand. However, his turn bet of 300 into a 500 pot is a significant sizing (60% pot). I am currently holding Ten-high with an inside straight draw (needing a 9). With only 4 outs to the straight (approximately 9% equity), I do not have the direct pot odds to call a 300-chip bet to win a total pot of 1100 (which would require about 27% equity). Since I have no showdown value and the board is paired, making it even more dangerous to chase draws, I will fold and preserve my stack.

K-Q offsuit is a premium holding in a heads-up match. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to build the pot and maintain aggression from the button. Claude has been active, but this hand is well ahead of an average defending range.

4-7 offsuit is a weak holding from the big blind. While Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament and may be light on this raise, calling out of position with such a marginal hand is not profitable. The pot odds (2:1) don't justify calling with a hand this weak, especially considering I'd be out of position for the entire hand. My stack is healthy at 20k+, so I can afford to fold and wait for better opportunities. Flash's aggression means I can find better spots to accumulate chips without playing marginal hands.

Raising with AJ from the button in heads-up play. This is a premium hand that plays well in position. A 2.5x BB raise (250 chips) applies appropriate pressure while maintaining good pot odds. Heads-up strategy favors positional aggression with quality holdings like AJ.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Claude, the Button, has raised to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds (150 to call into a 350 pot), Queen-Five offsuit is a marginal hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against an opponent who has shown consistent post-flop aggression in recent hands. I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to engage, rather than risk bleeding chips in a pot where I am likely to miss the flop or be forced to fold to a continuation bet.

I am holding 7h 3c on the button in a heads-up situation. While button ranges in heads-up play are traditionally very wide, 73o is among the weakest possible hands. Claude has a healthy stack and has been active. I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better spot rather than committing more chips with a bottom-tier hand.

Folding 7-3 offsuit on the button in heads-up play. While button ranges should be wide, this hand is too weak with poor post-flop playability and insufficient equity. Deep stacks allow for selective play of premium holdings from favorable positions rather than marginal spots.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Q6h. This is a standard opening hand in a heads-up situation, but against a player like Claude who has shown a tendency to play aggressively or defend when priced in, I want to keep the pot controlled or take the initiative. Raising is standard here to put pressure on the Big Blind and win the blinds. I will raise to 300.

Calling from the big blind with Ah-8h (Ace-eight suited). Flash raised to 300 from the button/small blind, a standard 3x raise. This is a solid hand with multiple ways to improve - I can make top pair, a flush, or even a straight. The pot is offering me 2:1 odds to call (200 into 400), and I'm deep stacked enough to play. Folding a decent hand would be exploitable, and raising out of position seems unnecessary with this specific hand. Calling allows me to see a flop and play my hand in position relative to how it connects with the board.

On the flop Td-4c-5h with Ah-8h, I have a powerful drawing hand with significant equity. I have an open-ended straight draw (13 outs: K, 6, and 7 hearts), plus flush draw potential, and two overcards. My hand strength is primarily in its future potential rather than current made strength. Since I'm first to act out of position, checking is optimal because: (1) It keeps the pot manageable while I'm drawing, (2) I can evaluate Flash's action and make an informed decision on future streets, (3) If Flash bets a reasonable amount (which they likely will given their aggressive tendencies), I can call with excellent pot odds given my 13 outs, and (4) If Flash checks back, I get a free card. This approach balances pot control with hand progression, which is the textbook play for strong draws out of position in a heads-up scenario.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a fairly dry board (Td 4c 5h). While I only have Queen-high, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet to take down the pot. Claude's check is typical, and a half-pot bet puts pressure on his range, which likely contains many hands that missed this flop entirely. If I am called, I still have two overcards to the 4 and 5, though I would likely give up on later streets unless the board improves my hand.

Calling 300 with Ah-8h on Td-4c-5h. I have a strong drawing hand with approximately 14-15 outs (8 flush outs + 6 non-flush straight outs + 3 aces + 3 eights). The pot odds are 3:1 (calling 300 into 900), which are favorable for a draw of this strength. I'm deep-stacked enough to play for value on the turn and river, and I'm getting nearly 2.5:1 on my money after accounting for my equity. Raising is suboptimal here because I'm primarily drawing rather than betting strong made hands, and doing so out of position would be unnecessarily expensive. Calling allows me to play positionally on later streets and maximize my equity.

Betting 700 on the turn with Ah-8h on Td-4c-5h-9d. I have approximately 24 outs (9 flush, 12 straight, 3 pair outs) giving me ~52% equity. This is a strong semi-bluff that applies pressure to Flash's range after their turn check. If called, I have excellent equity; if Flash folds weak holdings, I win the pot immediately. The bet size (58% of pot) is aggressive and well-justified by my draw strength. Stack sizes are healthy and support aggressive play here.

I am currently holding Queen-high with no significant draws on a board of Td-4c-5h-9d. After I continuation-bet the flop and was called, Claude has now led out for 700 on the turn. This 'donk lead' often indicates that the turn card helped the opponent or that they have a marginal made hand (like a Ten or a Nine) and want to take control of the betting. Given that I have zero showdown value and very little equity to improve, there is no reason to call or bluff into a player showing initiative. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Playing J4o on the button from small blind. While I have positional advantage, Jack-four offsite is a weak hand that doesn't justify further investment. I'm currently ahead in chip count (20,875 vs 18,975), so I don't need to take marginal spots. Flash has shown aggression throughout the tournament and maintains a competent playing style. Folding weak hands like J4o is disciplined poker - I should focus on playing stronger holdings from the button and exploiting position with better cards. With 55 hands remaining, I can be patient and selective.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit (Ks Jc), which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. Standard procedure is to raise for value and to take control of the hand with positional advantage. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament.

Defending the big blind in heads-up play with Q9o. Getting 2:1 odds to call and need to maintain a balanced BB defense range. Q9o has decent playability post-flop with potential to make straights. Calling allows me to see the flop without overcommitting, and I can adjust based on Flash's action and the board texture.

Defending my big blind call with Q9o on a Tc Ac 8h board. I have a gutshot straight draw (4 outs with K or J) but no made hand. Flash's check indicates weakness, and with marginal equity, checking is appropriate to keep the pot small and maintain flexibility. This allows me to control the pot size while potentially hitting on the turn or extracting information from Flash's turn action.

On this Ace-high board (Ac-Tc-8h), I do not have a pair, but I have a gutshot straight draw to the broadway straight (needing any Queen) and two overcards to the Ten and Eight. Claude checked to me, which is standard on an Ace-high board for the caller. I should continue with a semi-bluff to keep the initiative, as I can represent an Ace or a strong Ten, and I have back-door equity. A standard continuation bet of about half the pot (300) should suffice to either take it down now or set up later streets.

Folding Q9o on T-A-8 against Flash's 300 bet. With only 4 outs to a gutshot straight, I have roughly 15-18% equity, but need 25% to justify the call based on pot odds. The hand lacks showdown value and connectivity. In a spot where pot odds and equity significantly disconnect, folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button in a heads-up situation with 3-2 offsuit - normally a very weak hand, but position is crucial here. In heads-up play, hand standards are dramatically different, and the button/small blind position provides significant equity advantage. With a 20k+ chip stack and opponent's 19k+, I can apply pressure with a raise. A 2.5x raise to 250 applies reasonable pressure while I maintain fold equity and positional advantage. Even weak hands have decent equity in heads-up situations, and raising from the button with positional advantage is profitable strategy. I'm generating chip accumulation through position rather than hand strength, which is appropriate given the stage of the tournament (97 of 150 hands remaining).

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Six offsuit is a strong hand that is well ahead of a standard button opening range. Claude has been active, and while a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while out of position and see how the flop develops. I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 350).

I have top pair with a relatively weak kicker (Six). On an Ace-high board in a heads-up pot, this is a strong hand, but it is vulnerable to better Aces. Checking is the standard play here to control the pot size and allow Claude to bet with his wider range of bluffs or draws. I will look to call a standard continuation bet.

Flop continuation bet with made hand (pair of threes). Flash checked to me, indicating potential weakness. In heads-up play, betting maintains my preflop aggression and applies pressure. A 60% pot-sized bet (300) is standard for this situation with a made hand. My pair has decent value against his likely checking range, and this bet extracts value while remaining balanced.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively low board (A-4-3). While my kicker (Six) is weak, top pair is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Claude's bet of 300 into 500 is a standard continuation bet. I will call to see the turn, as raising might drive away his bluffs and only get called by better Aces or sets, while folding is far too tight given the strength of my hand.

On this turn, the deuce of diamonds completes a possible straight for any hand containing a five. Since I am out of position as the Big Blind in a heads-up match, I will continue with the strategy of checking to the pre-flop aggressor. My top pair remains a strong hand, but checking allows me to control the pot size and observe Claude's reaction to the board texture, which now includes various straight and flush draws.

Turn betting with two pair (threes and deuces) after Flash check. My hand improved significantly from the flop pair of threes. Flash's passive checking line suggests weakness. A ~55% pot-sized bet (600 into 1100) is standard value-extraction sizing on the turn with a strong made hand. This bet applies pressure while extracting value from Flash's likely weaker holdings (missed draws, weak pairs). The sizing maintains balance and avoids over-committing while still building the pot with my strong hand.

I currently hold top pair with an Ace on a highly coordinated board (4-3-A-2). The deuce on the turn is significant as any hand containing a five now has a straight. However, in a heads-up match, folding top pair to a single turn bet is generally too conservative, especially given the pot odds (calling 600 to win a total pot of 2300). Claude has been aggressive, and his range includes many hands I still beat, such as smaller pairs, heart draws, or diamond draws. Raising would likely only result in being called by hands that have me beaten (straights, two pair, or better kickers). Therefore, I will call to see the river, keeping the pot controlled while remaining in the hand.

The river completes a 5-high straight (A-2-3-4-5) on the board itself. With my Six of Spades, I hold a 6-high straight. However, this is a very dangerous board texture for several reasons: any Seven makes a 7-high straight, and since there are three diamonds on the board (Ad, 2d, 5d), any two diamonds in Claude's hand make a flush. Claude has been the aggressor on every street, which could indicate a strong hand, a draw that just got there, or a split pot situation. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only serves to build a pot I might lose. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size and see how Claude responds to the straight and flush possibilities.

River value bet with made hand (wheel straight A-2-3-4-5). Flash checked after calling the turn, indicating likely weakness. With a genuine straight, extracting value is essential. A 800 chip bet (~35% of the 2300 pot) is reasonable sizing that extracts value while remaining balanced. This hand wins against Flash's likely range of weak pairs, missed draws, and marginal holdings. In heads-up play at hand 97/150, generating chips through value extraction with strong hands is a key profitability mechanism.

The river completes a five-high straight on the board (A-2-3-4-5). With my Six of Spades, I hold a six-high straight (2-3-4-5-6), which beats any hand that only plays the board's straight. However, there are significant risks: any hand with a Seven makes a seven-high straight, and since there are three diamonds on the board, a flush is possible if Claude holds two diamonds. Claude has led the betting on every street, and his small river bet (800 into 2300) looks like a value bet. While I am vulnerable to a Seven or a flush, in a heads-up match, a six-high straight is too strong to fold for these odds (approximately 4:1). Raising is unwise as it only gets called by hands that beat me. Therefore, calling is the most balanced and appropriate decision.

Holding 8s 4h on the button in a heads-up situation, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range. While aggression is important in heads-up play, 8-4 offsuit has very little post-flop equity and Claude has shown a willingness to defend his big blind and play back. Given my slight stack lead and the quality of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and +EV long-term decision here to avoid being outplayed in a bloated pot with a weak hand.

Folding 8-3 offsuit on button/small blind. Despite getting favorable pot odds to call (only 50 more), the hand is too weak with poor post-flop playability. In heads-up play, while ranges are wider, 8-3o remains below the threshold of acceptable hands. Better opportunities will arise with stronger holdings against an aggressive opponent.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with T9o. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, especially with a chip lead of 21,325 to 18,525. My opponent, Claude, has shown both aggression and the ability to fold to pressure in previous hands. Raising here allows me to take control of the pot early or win it immediately. A standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I'm holding 6h3d, which is the worst hand in poker - unconnected low cards with no real playability. Flash has raised to 300 from the button in heads-up play, which is standard aggressive play given positional advantage. While we're in a 2-player situation where ranges are wider, 6-3 offsuit should be folded even in heads-up contexts. The hand has poor equity, is difficult to play out of position postflop, and I have much better opportunities coming. With fairly even stacks (Flash ~21k vs my ~18.5k), there's no desperate chip situation that would justify playing this weak hand. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

In heads-up play, button position is paramount. A6o has reasonable value against a wide BB range, we're getting 3:1 odds, and post-flop positioning will allow us to play profitably. This is a standard call that leverages both the positional advantage and decent hand value.

I am in the Big Blind with J7 suited. Claude has just limped from the Small Blind. J7s is a decent hand in a heads-up situation, but since there's no raise and I am in position for the rest of the hand (as the big blind in a 2-player game, actually I will be first to act post-flop in standard heads-up rules but here the button is the SB/Dealer), I'll check to see a flop for free. My stack is healthy and I don't need to inflate the pot with a marginal hand pre-flop against a limp.

On this low board of 3c-2d-5c, my J7 of spades hasn't connected directly, although I have two overcards. Claude limped from the small blind, which often indicates a wide but potentially weak range. Since I am first to act post-flop in this heads-up structure, I will check to see how Claude responds. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a free turn card if he checks back, preserving my stack in a small pot where I only have Jack-high.

Betting 125 chips on a dry flop with ace-high against a checking opponent. Flash's check on 3-2-5 indicates weakness. My positional advantage and hand strength warrant aggression to either take down the pot immediately or build it with the likely best hand. The small bet size maintains good risk-reward ratio and is consistent with my previous aggressive plays in similar spots.

In this heads-up situation, I checked the Big Blind with Jack-Seven of spades after Claude limped from the Small Blind. The flop of 3c-2d-5c is quite low and connected, providing several straight possibilities that favor a limping range. I checked, and Claude bet 125 into a 200-chip pot. I currently have only Jack-high with no direct draws and no spades on the board for my backdoor flush. While I have two overcards, they are not particularly strong on this texture, and calling would put me in a difficult position on the turn without improvement. Given the board texture and the lack of equity, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-high. Recent history shows Claude has been folding to pre-flop raises (Hands 100, 96, 90, 88). While Kh 3c is not a powerhouse, a standard 3x raise is often enough to take down the blinds pre-flop or allow me to take control of the hand. If called, I still have position and a high card that can win at showdown on dry boards.
Defending big blind in heads-up with 2:1 pot odds. J2o is weak but warranted a call given odds and position dynamics. Can win the pot post-flop with proper play on favorable board textures.

Checking with J2o on a K-Q-6 board. This is a weak hand with no made value, only backdoor flush possibilities. Checking allows me to control pot size, see Flash's bet (if any), and potentially check down to showdown if Flash also checks. Betting or raising would be speculatively weak and likely costly against an aggressive opponent. Checking is the disciplined defensive play that minimizes losses on a marginal hand.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a board of 6h-Kd-Qd. Claude checked to me, which is a standard play for the out-of-position player. Given that I have the best hand most of the time here, I should bet for value and to protect against various draws (diamonds or straight draws like JT). A bet of 300 into a 600 pot represents a standard continuation bet that can get called by a Queen, a 6, or diamond draws.

Folding J2o on the K-Q-6 board. While pot odds offer 4:1 (900:300), my hand has virtually no equity - no made hand, no straight draws, only negligible backdoor flush possibilities worth perhaps 15-20% equity. To be profitable with these odds, I'd need 5:1 or better. More critically, this is a multistreet decision: if I call, Flash will likely continue betting on the turn, forcing difficult decisions with a very weak holding. Against an aggressive opponent like Flash in heads-up play, this is a spot to fold disciplined. Stack preservation is important with 150 hands remaining, and J2o is simply too weak to justify continuing with suboptimal equity and poor hand showdown value.

Folding 9-4 offsuit from the small blind. While heads-up play suggests wider ranges, this hand is too weak to justify going to showdown against an aggressive opponent. With a slight chip deficit (18,275 vs 21,575), I should preserve my stack and wait for premium holdings. 9-4 has poor equity against Flash's likely calling range and will be difficult to play postflop.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with J7 offsuit in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Claude, is in the Big Blind. J7o is a marginal hand but generally strong enough to open from the button in a heads-up environment to apply pressure and maintain an aggressive image. I have a significant stack advantage (21,675 vs 18,175). A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take control of the pot early or win it pre-flop.

In heads-up play against Flash, calling with Q♠6♠ is justified given favorable pot odds (2:1 to call 200 to win 400 already in the pot). Flash's button raise could be a wide range in heads-up dynamics. While Q6o isn't premium, it has reasonable playability post-flop, and I can outplay Flash on many board textures. My position in the big blind allows me to see the flop affordably and maintain flexibility for the remainder of the hand. Stack sizes are relatively deep (18k effective), so I can play exploitatively post-flop.

Flash's check on the flop indicates weakness, which is a critical moment in heads-up play to apply pressure. My hand (Q♠6♠) currently holds queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (any King completes a straight), plus two spades for potential flush outs. The board (8♥J♥9♣) is relatively coordinated but Flash's check suggests they don't have a strong made hand or are playing cautiously. In heads-up dynamics, checking from position often signals weakness. I should bet here to take down the pot outright or build equity for a potential win. A bet of 250 (roughly 40% of the 600 pot) is appropriately aggressive—not overcommitting with my marginal holding while maximizing fold equity. Even if called, I have legitimate draws and can continue to apply pressure on subsequent streets. This action exploits the favorable spot created by Flash's passivity.

On a flop of 8h-Jh-9c, I have top pair (Jacks) with a seven kicker. This board is extremely coordinated, offering several straight and flush possibilities. Claude's lead of 250 into a 600 pot (a 'donk bet') often indicates a made hand that wants protection or a strong draw. While my kicker is weak, top pair in a heads-up pot is generally too strong to fold to a single small bet. However, raising here is risky as it could commit me to a large pot on a board where I could easily be behind a straight (QT, T7), two pair (J9, J8, 98), or a set. Calling allows me to maintain pot control, utilize my position, and see how the turn develops while keeping his potential bluffs or weaker draws in the hand.

Flash checked the turn after calling my flop aggression, indicating weakness in heads-up play. With queen-high and a gutshot straight draw, I should apply pressure by betting 500 (45% of the 1100 pot). This maintains my aggressive narrative from the flop, exploits Flash's checked weakness, and generates fold equity while building the pot profitably given my stack depth. Even if called, I retain legitimate equity with my straight draws and overcard.

The Jd on the turn is a very strong card for me, giving me three of a kind (Jacks). However, my kicker (seven) is quite weak. Claude has led out on both the flop and the turn, which is often a sign of strength on such a coordinated board. The board (8h-Jh-9c-Jd) makes several straights possible (QT, T7) and contains many better Jacks (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT) and full houses (J9, J8, 99, 88) that Claude could realistically hold. By calling instead of raising, I control the size of the pot and avoid being re-raised by a hand that has me dominated, while still keeping his potential bluffs and weaker made hands in the pot. I will evaluate the situation again on the river.

River bet for value with made hand (pair of Jacks from board + Q kicker). Flash's check suggests weakness after calling two streets of aggression. Betting generates fold equity while extracting value from weaker hands. Bet size of 700 (~1/3 pot) is standard for value betting with marginal showdown hands in heads-up play.

On a board of 8h-Jh-9c-Jd-4h, I have three of a kind (Jacks) with a seven kicker. Claude has led out on every street, which is a strong line on such a coordinated board. The river 4h completes potential heart flushes, and the board already contains several straight possibilities (QT, T7) and better Jacks (AJ, KJ, QJ, JT). However, in a heads-up game, three of a kind is a very strong hand, and Claude's river bet of 700 into a 2100 pot (1/3 pot) gives me excellent pot odds of 4:1 to call. Raising is too risky as I'm likely only called by hands that beat me, but folding is too tight given my hand's absolute strength and the price offered. I will call to see the showdown.

AcTs is a premium hand in heads-up play from the button. I should raise to seize the initiative, build the pot with a strong hand, and apply pressure. A 3x BB raise to 250 is standard and gives me good odds while maintaining positional advantage. This is well above the threshold for aggressive play from the button in heads-up situations.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 2d, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability post-flop. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button/Small Blind. While defending a wide range is important in heads-up play, 92o is at the very bottom of my range and is generally a clear fold against a raise, especially when out of position.

I am currently the chip leader in this heads-up match against Claude. I am on the button (Small Blind) and have been dealt 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While position is a significant advantage in heads-up play, there is no need to defend or attack with such a poor hand when I have a comfortable lead. Claude has shown a willingness to contest pots recently, so folding here and waiting for a better opportunity is the most disciplined play to protect my stack.

In heads-up play, I need to raise frequently from the button/SB to build pots and maintain initiative. QT is a solid broadway hand that's above my folding range. A 2.5x BB raise to 250 total applies appropriate pressure while being balanced with my typical range. Given my slight chip deficit, I need to play aggressively to accumulate chips.

In this heads-up scenario, I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Six of Clubs. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button (Small Blind). I need to call 150 more to see a flop. The pot odds are 3.33 to 1 (150 to win a total of 500), meaning I only need about 23% equity to continue. Ten-Six offsuit is a standard defending hand in heads-up play against a 2.5x open. Given my significant stack lead and the favorable pot odds, I will call to see a flop and evaluate the situation from there.

I have completely missed this flop of 9-3-5 with my Ten-Six offsuit. There are no straight or flush draws available to me, and my hand is currently just Ten high. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. I will evaluate Claude's action, but I am prepared to fold to almost any bet.

In heads-up play, continuation betting after a pre-flop raise is standard strategy, especially when opponents check. The flop (9d-3d-5s) is relatively dry and not heavily connected, which favors my aggressive play. I raised pre-flop to 250, and Flash called from the BB, checking this flop. QT is a solid broadway hand that can win many pots here. A bet of 350 (roughly 70% of pot) applies appropriate pressure—it's substantial enough to fold out weak holdings while keeping Flash's potential range uncertain. I'm in a slight chip deficit (16,425 vs 23,075), so maintaining aggression and initiative is crucial in heads-up play. This bet size allows me to build equity and pots when ahead while being appropriately sized relative to my remaining stack.

I have completely missed the flop with Ten-high and no significant draws on a 9-3-5 board. Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 350 into a 500 pot. This is a significant bet size (70% of the pot) and my hand has very little equity to continue. There is no reason to float or bluff-raise here given the lack of connectivity my hand has with this board. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation, preserving my substantial stack lead.

I have the best possible starting hand, Pocket Aces. My goal is to extract maximum value and build the pot. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate to induce action from the opponent while protecting my equity.

Calling Flash's button raise with QTo in heads-up. This is a standard defense range hand in 2-player poker. QT has decent equity and playability post-flop. With healthy stacks and 42 hands remaining, folding too often would allow blind erosion. The pot odds are favorable and I can leverage my late-position advantage post-flop. Flash's aggressive button opening is wide enough that my broadway hand is solidly profitable here.

Checking QT on a dry 9-3-2 flop after Flash's check. I have a strong draw (OESD with 8 outs) and queen high, making this a pot-control spot. With 42 hands remaining and deep stacks, I can see the turn cheaply while maintaining flexibility. Flash's check indicates weakness, and betting marginal hands in spots where I might get called by better hands or raised is not optimal. Checking allows me to realize my draw equity and keep the pot manageable.

I have the best possible starting hand (Pocket Aces) on a very dry, uncoordinated flop of 9-3-2 rainbow. My goal is to build the pot and extract value from any pair or high-card hands Claude might be holding. Since Claude checked to me, a standard continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) is a balanced way to solicit a call from marginal hands while maintaining control of the betting lead.

Calling Flash's 300 flop bet with QsTs on a 9h-3c-2s board. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to K or 8) plus two overcards. The pot odds are 4:1 which are favorable compared to the roughly 4.2:1 needed for a pure 8-out draw. In heads-up play with deep stacks and 42 hands remaining, calling allows me to see the turn while maintaining position and realizing my equity. Flash's modest bet sizing on a dry board suggests this is a reasonable spot to proceed.

Flash has checked the turn on a 9h-3c-2s-Kc board after I called their flop bet. I hold QsTs - I have Queen-high with an 8 still available to complete a straight (K-Q-T-9-8). Flash's turn check indicates weakness - they likely missed their draw or are proceeding cautiously. My Queen-high is likely ahead of most of their holdings, but betting into their check is marginal since I could get raised off the best hand or face a superior holding. Checking allows me to realize my draw equity, maintain flexibility, and see the river for free. This is optimal play with deep stacks in heads-up where pot control is valuable with marginal holdings. 42 hands remaining provides good fold equity management.

I am holding Pocket Aces, the strongest starting hand. The turn card is a King, which is an overcard to the flop but unlikely to have improved Claude's range more than mine, as I would also play AK or KQ this way. Claude has checked twice now, indicating weakness or a marginal hand like a Nine. I will place a value bet of 750 into the 1200 pot to continue building the pot and extract value from his pairs or potential draws.

Calling Flash's 750 turn bet with QsTs on 9h-3c-2s-Kc. I have Queen-high plus an 8-out open-ended straight draw (any J or 8 completes K-Q-T-9 straight). Pot odds of 2.46:1 are favorable against the ~4.2:1 required for a pure draw. In addition to draw equity, my current Queen-high likely has value against Flash's checking/betting pattern in heads-up play. With 42 hands remaining and deep stacks, calling maintains my flexibility while realizing full equity. Flash's modest turn bet sizing after checking the flop suggests I have reasonable current hand strength.

River action with K-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K). This is a premium made hand that wins against most of Flash's range. The turn King and river Jack completion gives me excellent showdown value. Betting 600 (roughly 22% of pot) extracts value from weaker holdings while avoiding overplaying. The only real vulnerability is to Aces which are in Flash's range but not the most likely holding given their flop check. Value betting ensures I maximize profit on this strong hand.

I have Pocket Aces, which is still a very strong hand despite the King and Jack appearing on the turn and river. Claude has lead out for 600 into a 2700 pot. This 'donk' bet often represents a marginal value hand like a King or a Nine trying to set a cheap showdown price, or occasionally a missed draw turning into a bluff. While it is possible Claude has a hand that beats me (like a set of 9s, 3s, or 2s, or two-pair like KJ or K9), the pot odds are excellent—I only need to be right about 15% of the time to justify a call. Raising is risky because it's unlikely a worse hand calls a raise, and I would be forced to fold to a re-raise. Calling is the most profitable and safest line to see the showdown.

3-9 offsuit is a marginal hand even from the button in heads-up play. While position is valuable and I only need to call 50 chips, 3-9o is below the threshold of playable holdings - it has poor connectivity, limited high card strength, and weak postflop playability. With nearly equal stacks (18,825 vs 21,025), I should be selective with my range and fold weak hands to preserve chips for premium situations. This is a standard fold.

In a heads-up situation, the button (Small Blind) should open a very wide range of hands. Ten-Four suited is well within the top 50% of starting hands and is a standard raise from this position to apply pressure and build a pot with a hand that has decent post-flop playability. A 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size throughout the match.

I'm holding pocket deuces (2h 2s) in the big blind. Flash raised to 300 from the button. Given the button's positional advantage and recent aggressive raising pattern, Flash likely has a wide range here. I'm getting 2:1 odds to call (200 to win 400), which is reasonable for a small pocket pair with set-mining potential. My stack is healthy at 18,725, and calling allows me to see a flop where my hand has showdown value and set potential. A 3-bet would be less effective here since my hand doesn't play well in a 4-bet pot, and calling gives me the best risk/reward ratio. If I hit my set, I win big; if I miss, I can evaluate the flop board texture and Flash's continuation play.

With pocket 2s on a coordinated flop (3s 6h Jh), I'm set mining with marginal current hand strength. Flash's check indicates potential weakness. Given the wet board texture with broadway cards and hearts, betting here is risky with just a pair of 2s. Checking allows me to see the turn card affordably - if I hit my set, I'll have a monster; if I miss, I can reassess based on Flash's action. This conservative play maximizes my risk/reward with a speculative hand.

On a Jack-high board like this, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. I hold the Ten of Hearts, giving me a backdoor flush draw to go with my overcard. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard here to apply pressure on Claude's range and potentially take the pot down immediately if he hasn't connected with the board.

Calling 300 with pocket 2s in a set-mining spot. While immediate pot odds are 4:1 against 8.5% hit probability, the implied odds are strong given Flash's aggression and stack depth. If I hit my set, Flash's range likely includes broadway hands and draws that will pay off. With 18,225 remaining after the call, I have ample room to fold if the turn develops unfavorably. Set mining is a standard profitable play with these stack sizes and board dynamics.

I'm holding pocket deuces (2h 2s) on a board of 3s 6h Jh 6d. My current hand is just a pair of 2s, which is extremely weak even though the board has paired. Flash checked to me on the turn, indicating potential weakness or a hand that's taking a cautious line.

With my marginal hand strength, a bet here would be speculative and likely results in losing when Flash has any reasonable holding (broadway pairs, overcards, or 6x combos for full houses). The pot is only 1,200 and my stack is healthy at 18,225.

My best option is to check and see the river card cheaply. If I catch another 2 (2 outs remaining), I'll have a very strong hand. If I miss, checking allows me to avoid bloating the pot with a weak holding and gives me information about Flash's river action. This conservative play maximizes my risk/reward ratio - I'm set mining with small equity but substantial implied odds if I do hit. Checking also keeps the pot small if Flash checks back, allowing a favorable showdown.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Six of Diamonds. Claude has checked to me for the second time. I currently hold a Ten-high flush draw. While a semi-bluff could be an option, checking back here is often the best play to realize equity for free. Claude's call on the flop suggests he has a piece of the board or a draw, and the pairing of the Six makes the board more dangerous if he was slow-playing a set or holding a Six. By checking, I ensure I see the river card and can potentially hit my flush or win at showdown if Claude is also on a draw.

Holding pocket deuces on a paired board (3s 6h Jh 6d Qs) gives me just one pair - an extremely weak holding. Flash's turn check indicated potential weakness. With no improvement on the river and marginal hand strength, betting here is not profitable. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply without bloating the pot. If Flash bet on the river, I can evaluate their bet size and stack dynamics before deciding whether to fold or call, but proactively betting with this weak holding doesn't make strategic sense.

On the river, the board is 3s-6h-Jh-6d-Qs. I have Ten-high (Th, 4h), having missed my heart draw. Claude has checked to me for the third time. While his repeated checking suggests weakness, he did call my continuation bet on the flop, indicating he likely has a pair (like a 3, a Jack, or a small pocket pair) or a better Ace-high. Since my hand has virtually no showdown value, I have to consider whether a bluff would be effective. However, the line of betting the flop, checking the turn, and then betting the river often looks like a missed draw to a competent opponent. Given that I have a healthy stack and it's early in the tournament's final stage, I will check and concede the pot rather than risking a large bluff against a range that called a flop bet on a Jack-high board.

Heads-up poker against Flash, and I'm on the button with Qc4h. While Q-4o is a weak hand in absolute terms, in heads-up play the hand ranges are much wider. I've already posted 50 for the small blind, and calling an additional 50 to see the flop is minimal risk given our relatively deep stacks (both around 195 BB). The cost-to-reward ratio is favorable here - I can see a flop and apply positional aggression if needed. Folding unnecessarily tightens me up too much in heads-up play, and raising would be overvaluing this weak hand. Best to call and play the flop accordingly.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). Claude limped from the Small Blind. In heads-up play, K9o is significantly stronger than a typical limping range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, as my hand has good high-card value and Claude's limp often indicates a weaker or more marginal holding.

Q4o is too weak to continue against a raise even in heads-up play. While I'm getting 2.6:1 odds on the call, this is a marginal hand at best with poor equity against Flash's raising range. In a tournament context at hand 111/150, I should be selective with my spots. Folding allows me to preserve chips for better opportunities where I have stronger holdings or better position. This is a clear fold.

I am on the button with 8s6d in a heads-up situation. While this is a mediocre hand, in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is key to controlling the pot and forcing the big blind to defend with marginal holdings. A standard 3x open (300 total) is appropriate here to either take the pot immediately or set up a post-flop continuation play, as I have the positional advantage.

I hold Ac-Tc (Ace-Ten of clubs), a premium hand in heads-up play. Flash has raised to 300 from the button. I'm getting 2:1 odds (need to call 200 to win 400 pot) with a strong holding that has good playability on most flops. Folding here would be unnecessarily tight. While I could reraise for value and fold equity, calling allows me to see the flop and potentially trap Flash if I hit, while also keeping the pot manageable. With roughly equal stacks (19,225 vs 20,375), pot control is reasonable here. A-T of clubs will have good equity against Flash's raising range heads-up, and I can make strong decisions postflop based on board texture.

Betting with strong drawing equity. A-Tc has approximately 14 strong outs (Q for broadway straight + any club for flush) giving me roughly 56%+ equity against Flash's wide button-raising range. This coordinated board doesn't necessarily favor Flash's random hands. Taking the initiative with 2/3 pot bet applies pressure while maintaining position and aggression in heads-up play. This bet sizing allows me to build the pot profitably with my draw while keeping the pot manageable.

I have 8-high on a flop of K-J-7 with a diamond draw. Claude has led for 400 into a 600 pot. My hand has virtually no equity and no real potential to improve into a winner against a range that likely includes Kings, Jacks, or strong draws. There is no reason to continue or attempt a bluff on this board texture after being led into.

Premium hand (ATs) in heads-up position on button. This is strong enough to raise and apply pressure. Standard 3x raise to 300 total chips (3 × 100 BB) is appropriate. This controls the pot, shows strength, and capitalizes on the hand's value both pre-flop and post-flop potential.

In heads-up play, Ace-five offsuit (A5o) is a strong starting hand. Claude has been active from the button, and my Ace serves as a significant blocker to many of his continuing hands. While calling is an option to see a flop, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and potentially end the hand immediately. I previously 3-bet Claude in Hand #87 with Ace-Jack and he folded, suggesting he respects pre-flop aggression. Given the stack depths (approx. 200 BBs), a raise to 900 is a standard sizing to put pressure on his opening range.

I have As-Ts, a strong suited ace that plays well postflop in heads-up situations. Flash 3-bet me from 300 to 900. In heads-up play, 3-bet ranges are much wider than in full ring, and ATs has excellent equity against Flash's likely holdings. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (1,200 to 900 = 2100:900, actually about 2.3:1), and ATs has well over 25% equity against Flash's wide range. Calling allows me to see a flop with a strong hand in position, where I can leverage postflop play. Folding would be too passive given the strength of my hand and the odds. A 4-bet/raise is possible but calling allows me to play in position and gather more information before committing more chips.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-five and was called. The flop of 3-7-2 rainbow is very dry and favors my perceived range as the 3-bettor. Claude's calling range consists of many broadway cards and small-to-medium pairs. While I only have Ace-high, I have a significant range advantage. A continuation bet of one-third the pot (600) is effective here to pressure his non-paired hands and maintain the initiative on a board where he is unlikely to have improved significantly.

With As-Ts on 3s-7h-2c, I have a premium draw with 13 outs (10 spades + 3 additional fours for straight). Two cards to come give me excellent equity. Raising to 1500 applies pressure on Flash's likely holdings (premium pairs or broadway draws) while maintaining proper risk/reward. The dry board and my position (button) favor aggression. This raise extracts value from weaker hands while building the pot when I have strong equity. Calling would be too passive given the strength of my hand as both a draw and potential bluff-catcher against air.

On this extremely dry board of 3-7-2 rainbow, Claude's raise over my continuation bet is a very strong signal. While I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw, his range for raising a 3-bet pot on such a disconnected flop heavily favors sets (77, 33, 22) or overpairs like 88-TT that he didn't 4-bet pre-flop. Although the price to call is 900 into a 3900 pot (requiring about 19% equity), I am out of position and my Ace might not even be a clean out if he has a set. I'll take the disciplined route and fold, preserving my stack for better spots.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with 7c 4h. This is a weak hand, but in a heads-up situation, I have the advantage of position. However, since the goal is to maximize my stack and Claude has shown aggression in previous hands (like the 3-bet in Hand #113), I will simply fold this trash hand and wait for a better spot, especially since I'm only slightly behind in stacks.

JdTd (Jack-Ten offsuit) in heads-up play on the button is a strong holding. With blinds at 50/100 and Flash having committed 100 as the big blind, I should apply pressure with this hand. In heads-up poker, position is critical, and JT has good playability with straight potential and decent top-pair possibilities. Raising here accomplishes multiple things: (1) it narrows Flash's range to stronger hands, (2) it gives me the initiative to control the hand post-flop, and (3) it allows me to potentially win the blind immediately. A raise to 250 is approximately 2.5x the big blind, which is standard aggressive play from the button in heads-up. This puts Flash in a spot where they must make a decision with whatever hand they're holding.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Six offsuit is a solid hand to defend from the Big Blind. Claude's raise to 250 is a standard opening size. I am getting favorable pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 500), and my hand has plenty of equity against a typical button opening range. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on the board texture.

On this paired board of Kc-7d-Ks, I have Ace-high. This board texture is quite dry and generally favors the pre-flop aggressor or the player who has a King. Since I am out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading here would rarely accomplish much against Claude's range. I will check and evaluate Claude's action; my Ace-high has significant showdown value if the hand stays small.

On the K-7-K flop, I hold JdTd—a strong semi-bluffing hand with an open-ended straight draw, flush draw, and 14+ outs. Flash's check indicates weakness, and in heads-up play, aggression is critical. A bet of 300 (approximately 60% of the pot) exploits Flash's weakness while maximizing both fold equity and value against weaker hands. If Flash raises, I have sufficient equity and pot odds to continue, and if Flash calls, I maintain control of the hand with significant drawing potential.

On a paired board of King-King-Seven, Ace-high retains significant value. This board texture is very dry, meaning it is difficult for Claude to have connected with it unless he holds a King or a pocket pair. Given the favorable pot odds and the likelihood that Claude is continuation-betting with a wide range of air or marginal hands, folding would be too tight. A call allows me to maintain the pot size while seeing a turn card to evaluate if Claude continues his aggression. My Ace-high often still has the best hand or at least enough equity to continue for one street.

On the turn, an Ace has arrived, giving me top pair. The board is currently Kc-7d-Ks-As. While I have improved to top pair, the presence of two Kings on the board makes it risky to lead into the pre-flop aggressor. If Claude has a King, I am drawing very thin. If Claude has a better Ace, I am outkicked. By checking, I allow Claude to decide whether to continue betting with his bluffs or marginal hands, or to check back and control the pot. Given that the Ace is a 'scare card' that might stop his bluffs, but also a card I hit, checking is the most prudent way to see how he reacts to this new board texture.

Betting 700 on the turn after Flash's check. JdTd has strong semi-bluff equity with an OESD (8 outs to make a straight with Q or 9). Flash's two consecutive checks suggest weakness. In heads-up play, continuing aggression here exploits Flash's apparent weakness while maintaining pot control. The bet is approximately 64% of the pot, consistent with my flop sizing. This either wins the pot immediately or puts me in a favorable spot with significant drawing equity if called.

On the turn, the Ace of Spades improves my hand to Two Pair (Aces and Kings). However, the board remains very dangerous. Any King in Claude's range (which he certainly has as the pre-flop aggressor) makes Trips or a Full House, and my Six kicker is very weak if Claude also holds an Ace. Claude's bet of 700 into a 1100 pot is a significant sizing. While I have improved, raising would likely only be called by hands that beat me (Kings or better Aces). Folding would be too weak given I have top pair on a paired board. I will call to see the river and decide based on the final board and Claude's final action, aiming to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

On the river Ten of Hearts, the final board is Kc-7d-Ks-As-Th. My best 5-card hand is Two Pair, Aces and Kings, with a Ten kicker (As-Ah-Ks-Kc-Th). My hole card (6c) does not play because the Ten on the board is a higher kicker. In this scenario, I am likely chopping the pot with any other Ax hand that has a kicker lower than a Ten (A2-A9). I lose to any King (Trips/Full House) and any Ace with a better kicker (AQ, AJ). Since I am out of position and my hand strength is marginal relative to Claude's range (which includes many Kings and better Aces as the pre-flop aggressor), checking is the only logical move. Betting would not fold out better hands and would likely only be called by hands that beat me or result in a chop.

Flash's three-street checking sequence after my consistent aggression indicates a medium-strength hand rather than a premium holding. With K-K-A-J-T, I have a strong hand that beats most of Flash's likely range (K-Q, K-J, A-Q, missed draws). A value bet of 900 (roughly 1/3 pot) extracts value from weaker hands, remains consistent with my aggressive line, and maintains balanced sizing. If Flash had a set or strong two pair, they would have check-raised on the turn when the Ace came. This bet size and action optimizes for value extraction while managing risk.

On a final board of Kc-7d-Ks-As-Th, my best hand is two pair, Aces and Kings, with a Ten kicker from the board (As-Ah-Ks-Kc-Th). My hole card (6c) is lower than the Ten on the board, meaning I will chop the pot with any of Claude's Aces that are ranked A2 through A9. I lose to any King (trips or full house) or any Ace with a Jack or Queen kicker. Claude's bet of 900 into a 2500 pot gives me pot odds of roughly 3.7:1, requiring me to be right (or chop) about 21% of the time to break even. Given the wide range of hands Claude can open from the button, including many weaker Aces that result in a chop and some potential bluffs, folding two pair in this heads-up situation would be too exploitable. I will call to realize my showdown value.

I have been dealt Pocket Queens, which is a premier starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build a pot and extract value from weaker hands in Claude's range. A standard opening raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a consistent play I've made throughout the tournament and should be respected while still encouraging action from a wide range of hands Claude might defend with from the big blind.

In heads-up play, K8o is a reasonable hand to defend with from the big blind against a button raise. The hand has decent equity against Flash's wide raising range. Rather than committing more chips with a 3-bet (which is marginal with K8o), I'll call to see the flop out of position but with reasonable hand strength. Pot odds support the call, and this keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to evaluate board texture.

On the Qs 4s Jc flop, I'm holding K8o from the big blind after calling Flash's preflop raise. This hand has moderate showdown value with the King as an overcard, but lacks any draws or made hand strength. With two broadway cards already on the board (Q, J), my hand is vulnerable to Flash's likely range. In heads-up play from out of position with a marginal hand, checking is the prudent play. This allows me to control pot size, gather information about Flash's hand strength through his action, and avoid committing additional chips with a hand that doesn't warrant aggression. If Flash bets, I can make a disciplined fold. If Flash checks back, I'll re-evaluate on the turn with more information.

I have flopped top set (Queens) on a board of Qs-4s-Jc. This is a monster hand, but the board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and some straight possibilities (T9, KT). Since Claude checked to me, I need to bet to build the pot and charge any potential draws or get value from a Jack. A bet of 400 into a 600 pot is a standard value bet size that protects my equity while inviting a call from weaker hands like Jx or spade draws.

Calling 400 on the Q♠ 4♠ J♣ flop with K♦ 8♠ from the BB in heads-up play. The 2.5:1 pot odds support defending this spot - my hand has approximately 25-35% equity against Flash's likely range (mix of value bets, medium pairs, and bluffs). While K8o is marginal, folding too frequently to flop aggression in heads-up play is exploitable. The King provides overcard equity and turn/river potential. By calling, I gather information about Flash's hand strength and remain positioned to make better decisions postflop. Given tournament stage (116/150) and stack depth, this is a balanced approach that doesn't overcommit with a marginal hand.

Checking the turn with K♦ 8♠ on a Q♠ 4♠ J♣ 7♣ board. K high is a marginal hand on this coordinated board. Checking allows me to reach the river at no additional cost while maintaining flexibility. This is the balanced, disciplined play that avoids overcommitting with a weak hand while preserving the ability to make river decisions based on Flash's action.

I have top set with pocket Queens on a board of Qs-4s-Jc-7c. This is an extremely strong hand, and since Claude called my flop bet, he likely has a Jack, a flush draw, or a straight draw (like T9). The 7c is relatively blank. My goal is to extract maximum value and protect against draws. I will bet 1,000 into the 1,400 pot to build the pot for a potential river shove and to charge Claude's draws or get value from his Jacks.

Calling 1000 on the turn with K♦8♠ on Q♠4♠J♣7♣. Pot odds of 2.4:1 require ~29% equity; my hand has approximately 25-35% equity against Flash's typical turn-betting range. With one card to come, a King overcard, and Flash's demonstrated aggressive tendencies (which often include semi-bluffs), this is a disciplined call that maintains balance. Folding would be exploitable in heads-up play. The expected value is marginally positive, and I preserve stack flexibility for river play.

River check with K♦8♠ on Q♠4♠J♣7♣5♦. This is King-high with no kicker against an opponent who has bet decisively on flop (400) and turn (1000). The 5♦ didn't improve my hand, and my King-high equity is minimal against Flash's likely range of pairs and strong holdings. Bluffing here is poor - Flash's aggressive line suggests real hand strength. Checking preserves chips, avoids further losses, and allows Flash to either check back (unlikely) or bet again (where I can fold). This is the disciplined, bankroll-preserving decision given my weak holding.

I have the nuts (top set of Queens) on a board of Qs-4s-Jc-7c-5d. There are no possible straights or flushes, and the only hands that could potentially be calling are Jacks or perhaps a smaller set like 77 or 44, both of which I beat. Since Claude checked to me, I must bet for value. A bet of 2,500 into the 3,400 pot is a strong sizing that aims to extract maximum value from any Jack-high hands or sticky mid-pairs in Claude's range while appearing like a continuation of my aggression throughout the hand.

On the river with K♦8♠ facing Flash's 2500 bet into a 5900 pot, I need approximately 30% equity to call (2500:8400 = 1:3.36). King-high is the weakest showdown hand I can possibly have. Flash's aggressive line—betting 400 on the flop, 1000 on the turn, and now 2500 on the river—indicates a made hand rather than a pure bluff. The blank 5♦ didn't improve my hand at all. My King-high realistically has only 15-25% equity against Flash's range of paired holdings, straights, or strong broadway combinations. I've been disciplined throughout this hand by controlling pot size and avoiding overcommitment with a marginal holding. Calling here with such weak equity and strong betting action against me violates good bankroll management. With a healthy stack at 17325 (nearly tied with Flash's 16775) and 34 hands remaining, folding preserves chips for better spots. This is a clean fold.

With pocket 3s on the button in heads-up play, I should be aggressive from position. Raising to 250 (2.5x the big blind) applies pressure on Flash, builds the pot in a hand where I have positional advantage, and forces him to make a decision. This is standard heads-up strategy - applying aggression from the button with a playable hand. While pocket 3s isn't premium, position and the dynamic of forcing my opponent to act first post-flop make this a clear raise.

I am currently the chip leader with a comfortable margin after winning a significant pot in the previous hand. Claude has raised to 250 from the button/small blind. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 500), 3h 2h is the absolute bottom of the starting hand range. Even though it is suited, its high-card value is non-existent and it is easily dominated. Playing this hand out of position against an aggressive opponent like Claude is likely to result in a loss of chips over the long run. Given the tournament stage (Hand 117), I prefer to wait for better opportunities to use my chip lead rather than defending with the weakest possible hands.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit (As Jc), which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build the pot and maintain aggression. Raising to 300 is a standard open-raise size that puts pressure on the big blind while allowing me to play a controlled pot in position if called.

I'm in the big blind with 5-6 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Flash has raised to 300 from the button, and while Flash has been aggressive throughout the tournament, my hand lacks sufficient equity to defend here. With 32 hands remaining and a healthy 17,325 chip stack, I should preserve chips by folding marginal hands and wait for premium holdings or better situations. Defending with 5-6o against an aggressive opponent would require profitable equity, which I don't have in this scenario.

Heads-up button play requires wider ranges. While 5-4o is weak in isolation, position is critical in heads-up poker. A raise to 150 (3x the 50 to call) is standard sizing that applies pressure on Flash's big blind and either wins the pot immediately or allows me to play post-flop with position. This is more effective than a speculative call or a weak fold.

In a heads-up situation, King-high (K3o) is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a button raise. The pot odds are very favorable, as I only need to call 100 more to win a total pot of 300, requiring only 25% equity. Claude's 2x raise is small, and I should see a flop with high-card value even if the kicker is weak.

In this heads-up pot, I am out of position as the Big Blind. I have King-high on a board of Queen-Nine-Seven with two hearts. This board texture likely favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Claude), as it contains many high cards and drawing possibilities. Since I have no made hand and very little equity, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will see how Claude reacts before deciding whether to continue in the hand.

Betting 250 (0.625x pot) applies solid pressure in a heads-up situation where Flash has shown weakness by checking. Even with 54o, the fold equity is significant given my position and preflop aggression. This sizing is standard for continuation betting and either wins the pot immediately or allows me to reassess on the turn.

On a coordinated board of Q-9-7 with two hearts, my King-high has very little equity against Claude's range, which includes many pocket pairs, high cards, and draws. Claude is betting about 60% of the pot, and without even a backdoor flush draw or a straight draw, continuing out of position is a losing play. I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better spot.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation. This is one of the strongest starting hands in poker, especially in a 1v1 scenario. I should raise to build a pot and maintain aggression. A standard raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here to extract value and put pressure on the big blind.

Folding 5-4 offsuit from the big blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker with minimal winning potential. While Flash is aggressive from the button/small blind, I'm out of position post-flop and this hand doesn't have enough equity to warrant a call against a raise. With 30 hands remaining and reasonable stack depth, there's no reason to play marginal/trash hands. Better to preserve chips and wait for stronger opportunities.

In heads-up play (hand 121 of 150), I'm on the button with 3d 4d - one of the weakest possible hands. While position is normally valuable in heads-up poker and wider ranges are standard, 3-4 is virtually unplayable. It has no real equity advantage post-flop against most of Flash's continuing range, and while folding the button means conceding the small blind, preserving chips with marginal hands is important late in the tournament. With 29 hands remaining and a reasonable chip stack (17,375), I should fold this and wait for better opportunities where I can accumulate value.

In a heads-up situation, 85o is a marginal hand. While standard heads-up strategy often dictates raising a wide range from the button, I have a significant chip lead over Claude and have consistently folded weak starting hands from this position in recent orbits. I will continue this disciplined approach and fold this hand to wait for a more advantageous starting position.

As8s is a premium holding in the small blind of heads-up play. This hand has significant equity with top ace, eight kicker, flush potential, and wheel straight potential. In heads-up small blind, I should be raising with strong hands to apply pressure and build the pot when I have a strong range advantage. A raise to 250 (2.5x the big blind) is appropriately sized - strong enough to apply meaningful pressure without overcommitting with a hand that, while strong pre-flop, still needs to improve on most flop textures. This also maintains flexibility for post-flop play and aligns with standard heads-up small blind strategy.

I am holding Ad 6d in the Big Blind. This is a very strong hand for heads-up play, especially against a standard button open of 2.5x. Claude has been relatively active, and A6s is well ahead of his wide opening range. While I could 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot, calling allows me to keep his range wide and use my stack advantage to navigate the flop. However, given the stage of the tournament and the effectiveness of Ace-high suited hands, I will call the 150 to see a flop, keeping the pot manageable while retaining strong playability.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace and a 6 kicker on a relatively dry board. In a heads-up situation after calling a pre-flop raise from the big blind, checking to the aggressor is the standard strategic move. This allows me to see how Claude reacts to the Ace-high board and keeps his wide opening range in the pot. If he bets, I have a very easy call given my hand strength, and checking also protects my overall checking range.

I have a premium holding (As8s) that has flopped top pair with the Ace on an A-3-2 board. My hand has significant equity including: top pair (Ace), flush draw (9 outs), potential straight draws, and an 8 kicker. Flash checked from the big blind, indicating weakness or a draw. In heads-up play, I should apply pressure with my strong holdings. A bet of 250 (0.5x pot) is appropriately sized to generate value from weaker hands like King-Queen, pocket pairs, or draws while maintaining flexibility for future streets. Checking here would give Flash a free card to improve and doesn't extract value from my hand advantage. The bet applies the pressure necessary to leverage my superior range strength in this heads-up situation.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace and a 6 kicker on a very dry board (A-3-2 rainbow). In heads-up play, this is a very strong hand. Claude's lead of 250 into a 500 pot is a standard continuation bet that he would make with a wide range of hands, including bluffs, pocket pairs, or smaller pieces of the board. While I could raise for value, calling is more strategic here as it keeps his bluffs in the pot and prevents me from being isolated against a better Ace (like AJ-AK) or a set. By calling, I can re-evaluate on the turn while keeping the pot manageable.

I have top pair with an Ace, but the turn card (4c) makes the board very coordinated, completing a potential straight for any hand containing a 5 (A-2-3-4-5). Since I am out of position and Claude was the initial aggressor, checking is the most strategic play here. This allows me to control the pot size, see how Claude reacts to the straight-completing card, and avoid being forced into a large pot with a hand that might now be second-best. If he checks back, it indicates he might be pot-controlling or lacks a 5; if he bets, I can evaluate the price and his range to decide on a call.

Top pair with an excellent 9-card flush draw on a coordinated board. Flash's turn check indicates weakness or a draw. In heads-up play, I should apply pressure with my strong holdings. A bet of 450 (roughly 45% of the 1,000 pot) is appropriately sized to extract value from weaker hands, draws, and medium pairs while maintaining aggression. This prevents Flash from taking a free card and leverages my superior hand equity.

I am holding top pair with Ace-Six on a board of A-3-2-4. The turn card (4c) is significant as it completes a potential straight for any hand containing a 5 (A-2-3-4-5). Claude's bet of 450 into a 1000 pot is relatively small (under half pot), suggesting he might be betting for value with an Ace, a 5, or perhaps a lower pair/bluff. While the straight is a concern, my top pair is too strong to fold to such a small bet. Raising here doesn't achieve much other than isolating me against a 5 or a better Ace. By calling, I keep the pot manageable, retain Claude's bluffs in his range, and can evaluate the river card to see if the board pairs or if a 5 appears for a potential chop. I will call the 450.

On the river, the board has paired with a second Trey (3s), and the board now shows Ac-3h-2s-4c-3s. My hand is Aces and Treys with a Six kicker. The board is quite dangerous as any 5 in Claude's range completes a straight (A-2-3-4-5), and the pairing of the 3 makes trips or a full house possible for hands like A3, 43, or 23. Since I am out of position and have been in check-call mode, checking is the most strategic option. It allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a showdown. If Claude checks behind, I may win against a weaker Ace or a missed draw. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the bet size and his perceived range to decide on a call, but leading out myself would likely only result in being called by hands that have me beaten.

River value bet with top pair. Flash's check pattern (flop, turn, and river) indicates a marginal hand or weakness. With A-8, I beat most of Flash's realistic range including weaker Aces and smaller pairs. A 900 chip bet (~47% of current pot) is appropriately sized to extract value from hands like AK, AQ, AJ, AT, A9, and pocket pairs like JJ-66, while still offering reasonable odds for Flash to call if holding any of these hands. This maintains aggression and value extraction in heads-up play while respecting that Flash may have improved on the river.

I am holding top pair (Aces and Treys with a Six kicker) on a highly coordinated board: Ac-3h-2s-4c-3s. Claude has been the aggressor throughout the hand. On the river, the board paired the 3, which is significant. While my hand is technically top pair, the 6 kicker is extremely weak. In Claude's range, any 5 (making a straight), any 3 (making trips or a full house), or any Ace with a kicker higher than 6 (AK, AQ, AJ, AT, A9, A8, A7) has me beaten. The only hands I realistically beat that he might be betting for value are A2 and A4, which are now downgraded to Aces and Treys with a lower kicker than mine due to the board pairing. However, it is more likely that a three-street bet from Claude represents a 5, a 3, or a strong Ace. Given the pot odds and the high likelihood that I am behind, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my significant stack advantage.

I am on the button with 7d 6d, which is a strong speculative hand (suited connectors). In heads-up play, this is a clear raising hand to take the initiative. I will make a standard raise to 3x the big blind.

Calling with A2o in heads-up from the big blind against Flash's button raise. This hand has reasonable showdown value and I'm getting 2:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, Flash's raising range is wide enough that folding is too passive. Raising with this specific hand is not profitable, so calling to see the flop and evaluate post-flop is the optimal play.

Checking with A2o on a dry, paired flop (5s-5c-8c) in heads-up play. My ace is likely best against Flash's wide button range, but the kicker is weak and a paired board diminishes hand strength. Checking allows me to control pot size with this marginal hand while seeing Flash's action. If he bets, I can make an informed decision based on bet size and relative stacks. If he checks, I get a free card to improve. This is more profitable than betting/bluffing with such a weak holding or folding face-up weak value.

On a flop of 5s-5c-8c, holding 7d-6d gives me a double gutshot straight draw (any 4 or 9 completes a straight). This is a strong semi-bluffing candidate. Since Claude checked to me, I will continue with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain initiative and potentially win the pot immediately if he has missed the board.

Folding with A2o against Flash's flop bet on a paired board. While pot odds are reasonable at 4:1, my hand has minimal showdown value. The deuce is a poor kicker and the paired board diminishes any ace value significantly. Flash's betting line suggests either genuine strength or a wider semi-bluff range, but either way, A2o is too weak to continue. Preserving chips with this marginal hand is superior to calling with such weak showdown equity, especially when hit rates are low and kicker problems are significant when an ace does arrive.

Heads-up button play with positional advantage. Even with a weak hand like 4d7d, raising to 200 (150 more) applies pressure and allows me to win the pot preflop through fold equity. The button/SB role is crucial in heads-up poker, and aggressive play from position with moderate stack depth is standard strategy. This raise size is appropriate relative to the 100 BB and pot, and exploits Flash's likely decision point.

In a heads-up situation, King-high is a relatively strong starting hand. Claude has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning I only need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot odds (3:1) are excellent, and K4o is well ahead of a typical button raising range. I will call to defend my big blind and evaluate the flop.

On this coordinated flop of 6h-8h-5c, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to complete a 4-5-6-7-8 straight) and a backdoor flush draw with the King of Hearts. Being first to act in this heads-up post-flop situation, checking is the standard play to evaluate Claude's reaction and hopefully see a cheap or free turn card.

Heads-up continuation bet with a straight draw. Flash checked from BB, indicating weakness. I have positional advantage and should maintain aggression. 4d7d has 8 outs to a straight plus backdoor draw potential. A 280 bet applies pressure, generates fold equity, and values my draw. If Flash calls, I still have two more streets to hit my draw or make a decision. The bet size is appropriately balanced for pot control while maintaining aggression.

On this highly coordinated board (6h-8h-5c), I have King-high, a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7), and a backdoor flush draw with the King of Hearts.
